This research explores the role of memory type in opioid-mediated reinforcement using a fear-based active avoidance memory task. Male Sprague Dawley rats were trained on the paradigm, receiving immediate post-training injection of heroin or saline. Conditioned behavior was assessed after 8 training sessions. Clear behavioral differences motivated a control group separation into 'freezers' and 'avoiders'. Heroin treated animals displayed conditioned avoidance and non-responsivity significantly different from avoiders but not freezers, suggesting that post training heroin administration counteracted avoiding and facilitated freezing. This contrasts prior findings of object and social recognition memory enhancement at equivalent heroin doses, implicating the relevance of memory type in determining the modulatory effects of post-training opioids, and that administration inhibits memory by biasing competition between cognitive strategies.
author: Harris, Cailean.

title: Molecular Characterization of Recalcitrant Soil Organic Matter in an Organic Soil Profile.
abstract: The soil organic carbon (SOC) pool is the largest carbon store in the biosphere, containing over 1500 Gt C up to a depth of one meter. This amount exceeds the carbon in both the atmosphere and vegetation. Muck soils, a form of an organic soil, contain high organic C and moderate to high mineral content, and has a unique hydrology in the soil profile. Soil organic matter that is recalcitrant persists for hundreds to thousands of years and is a major C pool in the biosphere. Its relative resistance to biochemical decomposition is due to its molecular chemistry which involve formation of stable molecules, such as fused aromatic ring structures (FAR) and carboxyl-rich alicyclic or aliphatic molecules (CRAM/CCAM). These structures increase recalcitrance by limiting water solubilization, specifically due to their hydrophobicity. Characterization at the molecular level of these structures has long been hindered by the insensitivity of Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) techniques due to low 13C abundance in soils so we studied a carbon-rich organic soil. We sampled three of the soil horizons-organic-rich Of and Om, and a gleyic clay soil layer Cg with total organic carbon content of 45%, 42%, and 2%, respectively. Advanced solid- and solution-state NMR and Diffuse Reflectance Infrared Fourier Transform (DRIFT) spectroscopy were used analyze humic and humin fractions derived from alkaline extraction and from soil. Both NMR and DRIFT spectra provided evidence of the formation of FAR and CRAM/CCAM structures. In Cg horizon samples, we detected further processing of FAR structures and the possible preservation and further processing of recalcitrant soil organic matter (rSOM) under anaerobic conditions. DRIFT spectroscopy with peak identification was used to examine the early diagenesis of soil organic matter. This approach may pave the way for studying FAR and CRAM structures due to its speed, cost-effectiveness, and simplicity. Study of soil samples extracted with polar acetonitrile (CAN) and non-polar n-hexane (HEX) solvents revealed unique compositional properties of CRAM moieties in the Cg horizon, implying microbial reworking and radical-assisted synthesis of biomolecules/precursors to form CRAM. The findings provide essential insights into the molecular characterization of rSOM and its role in long-term carbon sequestration in terrestrial soil ecosystems.
author: Gamage, Jeewan.

title: Molecular Investigation of Exogenous Stages of Eimeria tenella: Expression of Fragmented Mitochondrial rDNA, Organellar Genome Dynamics, and Development of a Transcriptional Activity-Based Assay for Measuring Oocyst Viability.
abstract: Eimeria tenella is one of several Eimeria spp. that cause coccidiosis, a cosmopolitan disease with major impacts on the commercial poultry industry. Once sporulated, oocysts greatly reduce metabolic activity and can withstand chemical and mechanical assault. These features support parasite environmental persistence and have obfuscated research of this portion of the lifecycle. The work described here was conducted with the aim of illuminating the 'black box' that represents the eimerian oocyst. Expression of the fragmented mitochondrial ribosomal DNAs (rDNA) of E. tenella was confirmed via Next-Gen sequencing. Discrete expression of 45 mitochondrial features, including 18 of 19 previously annotated putative rDNA was observed. Polycistronic expression and varying degrees of rRNA polyadenylation were detected. The range of specific rRNA abundances and low abundance of polycistronic transcripts suggest transcription occurs via modes other than purely polycistronic. Confirmation of rDNA expression and characterization of polyadenylation and relative abundances of non-coding (putatively ribosomal) RNA from the mitochondrial genome of E. tenella improve mitogenome annotation and lead to further veins of questioning. Abundance of organellar genomes during and following sporulation was examined via qPCR. Organellar genome abundance did not hold stoichiometry with the well-described four-fold increase of nuclear DNA. Mitogenome abundance increased approximately 3.3-fold over the course of sporulation. Apicoplast DNA increased ~1.27-fold over a period that extended slightly beyond the morphological completion of sporulation. Characterization of organellar genome copy numbers during exogenous development supports understandings of the roles of these endosymbiotic organelles and improves the value of these genomes as molecular quantification targets. Several candidate targets for a qPCR-based viability assay were identified via transcriptome sequencing. Increase in abundance of these targets in response to heat stimulation was compared to fecundity in oocyst sub-samples taken over a period that encompassed total or major loss of viability. One transcript showed an increase in abundance that correlated highly with fecundity and that showed a wide dynamic range: an ideal assay target. A beta version of a molecular viability assay was tested to closer achieve the goal of immunological control of coccidiosis, with additional potential uses in management of other coccidial diseases.
author: Kruth, Perryn.

title: Molecular and Physiological Mechanisms Underpinning the Changes in Plant Growth and Development in Arabidopsis thaliana Exposed to Low Red: Far-Red Light as a Competition Cue.
abstract: Low red to far-red (R:FR) ratios of light act as a competition cue prior to direct competition for resources. Upon detection of low R:FR light by phytochromes, a suite of signalling events occur that lead to changes in the morphology and physiology of the plant. Many of the molecular mechanisms and signalling events that lead to these changes remain to be elucidated. The main objective of this research was to further understand the molecular and physiological mechanisms underpinning the response to resource-independent weed competition from light detection to altered plant growth. This research provides important insights into the biological function of singlet oxygen (1O2) in Arabidopsis under resource-independent weed competition. The biological function of 1O2 as a signalling molecule under this condition appears to be dependent on the repression of superoxide (O2-) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production, which occurs through dramatic alterations in activities of O2- and H2O2 scavenging enzymes (superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase) and the redox status of ascorbate and glutathione that balance the redox poise. The specificity of 1O2 signaling is demonstrated by minimal overlap and differences in whole transcriptomic responses between Arabidopsis under low R:FR light and other plant 1O2 generating systems. Upregulation of the 1O2-responsive ILL6 along with ST2a, which act as negative regulators of jasmonate (JA) accumulation and response, connect 1O2 signalling to the shade avoidance response observed under low R:FR. This connection provides important clues about how the growth inhibitory effect of 1O2 through the attenuation of JA-mediated responses may be exploited by plants under low R:FR light to control rapid elongation growth in anticipation of impending competition.
author: Berardi, Nicole.

title: Morphological and Genetic Dynamics in Cannabis sativa L.: Transcriptomic Insights into Leaf Morphogenesis, Phase Transition, and Somatic Embryogenesis.
abstract: This project aimed to determine mechanisms governing leaf morphogenesis, phase transition, and somatic embryogenesis in Cannabis sativa L. (cannabis). A comprehensive investigation into the genetic and morphological dynamics shaping these critical processes in cannabis, focusing on the White Widow cultivar as a model system is presented. Leaf development in cannabis plants exhibits a distinct pattern from germination, characterized by a node-specific increase in serrated leaflets, followed by a subsequent decrease, unveiling a transition from vegetative to reproductive phases. A comparative transcriptomic analysis unveiled 1358 differentially expressed genes, notably implicating genes like YAB, AGO5, and TCP4 in leaflet formation and morphology. Additionally, genes associated with phase transition and flowering, such as SPLs, ELFs, SOC1, and CEN-Like, displayed specific expression patterns during these transitions. The study delved further into the alterations in transcriptome profiles across nodes, displaying opposite and alternate phyllotaxy during the phyllotactic transition. Among 17293 expressed genes, 6878 were identified as differentially expressed, emphasizing transcription factors associated with shoot apical meristem development and key hormone signaling pathways, particularly auxin and cytokinin, which are pivotal in phyllotactic variations. Genes known for their roles in phyllotaxy alterations in Arabidopsis, including LAX, PIN, ARF, YUC, and CLV3, exhibited distinct expression profiles during phyllotactic changes in cannabis. Furthermore, the investigation into cannabis calli, distinguishing between non-embryogenic and embryogenic types, highlighted 6018 genes with significant differential expression. Notably, phytohormone-related genes and various transcription factors exhibited distinct expression patterns between the callus types. Epigenetic-related genes, such as histone deacetylases, chromatin remodeling factors, and EMBRYONIC FLOWER 2, showed differential expression, notably impacting the transition from embryogenic callus to somatic embryogenesis by influencing the repression of key genes like LEC2, AGL15, and BBM. This comprehensive analysis provides profound insights into the genetic regulation of leaf morphogenesis, phase transition, and somatic embryogenesis in cannabis. Together, these studies offer crucial insight that extends to different fields related to cannabis, including optimization of cultivation, developmental biology, and biotechnological applications.
author: Hesami, Mohsen.

title: Mosaic: The Capacity of Multi-use Landscapes in Southern Ontario to Maintain Biodiversity and Ecosystem Service Delivery.
abstract: While research and policy have recognized the importance of conserving biodiversity, the rapid and continued expansion of urban areas across the globe hinders conservation efforts, particularly as many areas with high biodiversity are found in landscapes already modified by human use. These human-impacted areas frequently result in mosaic landscapes with fragments of multiple landcover types. While researchers have a strong understanding of the direct impacts of urbanization, there is still a rudimentary understanding of how changes landcover may indirectly affect natural areas in mosaic landscapes. Using the Credit Valley Watershed as a case study, I seek to uncover the impact that landcover composition and configuration has on the biodiversity of forest understory communities of conservation forests. I combined geospatial data with plant monitoring data to determine 1) the impacts of landscape composition on plant species diversity, 2) impacts of land-use composition and configuration on plant functional communities and, 3) the ways plant functional traits of an ecosystem can be scaled up to measure ecosystem service delivery. The first study found significant species-level and community biodiversity changes associated with landcover, likely impacting the forest's potential to support biodiversity. The second study found that landscape variables were associated with significant trait-divergence-more functional diversity than would be expected at random-with the functional trait dynamics appearing dependent on landscape variables. And finally, a literature review of plant functional trait's relationship to ecosystem services found strong evidence of trait-service relationships, with some trait-service relationships consistent across ecosystems and conditions, and others appearing to be dependent on local conditions. These findings indicate that the plant understory communities of the Credit Valley conservation forests are not random assemblages, and the communities are associated with the landscape around each site. These forest communities are shaped by the indirect impacts of the surrounding landscape as well as by the specific and direct environmental factors of each forest. Since the community functional traits have been established as a foundation of ecosystem service delivery - via their effect on ecosystem processes and general ecosystem function - the landscape also likely impacts the capacity of southern Ontario forests to deliver ecosystem services.
author: Miedema Brown, Liane.

title: Moving Through the Cis-tem: A Collection of Digital Stories Exploring Racialized Trans and Non-binary Experiences Navigating the Canadian Health Care System.
abstract: 'Moving Through the Cis-tem' is the first qualitative study that was conducted by and for racialized transgender and non-binary people navigating the health care system. Grounded in the collective work of queer and trans community of colour, this project aims to creating more space for people to share their stories; to unearth what care looks like for those whose identities are at multiple marginalized intersections; and to investigate what it means to honour and respect peoples' bodies. When we talk about gender-affirming care, we are actively resisting the medical discourses of what it means to be trans and non-binary. Three digital stories came out of this project and will be shared as a form of knowledge mobilization. This study is a direct continuation into the collective work of improving and advocating for racialized transgender and non-binary people to have access to all the types of care and support they need; to be heard and validated by health care providers; and to share their stories and lived experiences as they navigate through the health care system.
author: Sookpaiboon, Skylar.

title: Multi-Omics Identification of Yield Limiting Factors in Molecular Pharming.
abstract: Biologics, including protein-based drugs, are among the fastest growing categories of therapeutics under development today. While most biologics are made using mammalian cell systems, plant-based production systems provide a cost-effective approach to making new and similar medicines to fight disease. This process, called molecular pharming, uses a common plant pathogen, Agrobacterium tumefaciens, to deliver genes encoding for a specific protein product to the plant host, Nicotiana benthamiana. Although plant systems offer excellent potential for therapeutic protein production, barriers, such as plant host defense response, impact yield and limit wider adoption of the technology. In this thesis, I examine the molecular pharming pipeline from a systems-biology perspective to identify putative yield limiting factors. First, I explore the utilization of bioreactors as a substitute for shake flasks in bacterial biomass production. I examine variations in bacterial growth across the tested parameters and establish conditions necessary for consistent bacterial culturing. Through proteomic profiling of cultures under each growth condition, I identify distinct and growth-specific responses in bacterial protein abundance shedding light on the functional roles of these proteins that may influence bacterial processes involved in successful agroinfiltration and transformation. Next, I investigate the impact of time on bacterial culture preparation in agroinfiltration medium. I reveal distinct protein profiles associated with different bacterial treatment conditions and exposure timings, particularly in relation to pathogenesis, motility, and nutrient acquisition systems. These results indicate a progressive cellular remodeling of the bacterium over time to highlight the interconnectedness of pathogenesis and chemotaxis-related proteins with transport and metabolism. Finally, I investigate the infectome of N. benthamiana and A. tumefaciens to reveal distinctive patterns of bacterial protein production associated with each growth condition, which directly impact plant defense responses and the production of the target protein over time. By combining proteomic and metabolomics profiling, I detect the fluctuation of secondary metabolite production throughout the infection process, providing new insights into the intricate modulation of the dual biological systems. My findings suggest that bacterial growth in bioreactors may facilitate the evasion of early plant defense responses against A. tumefaciens. Overall, this thesis contributes to the knowledge of molecular pharming and identifies specific factors that may limit the yield of recombinant proteins.
author: Prudhomme, Nicholas.

title: Multi-Scale Model Formulation of a Porous Medium Biofilm Reactor and the Effects of Diffusion, Attachment, and Detachment on Reactor Performance.
abstract: We derive a one-dimensional macroscopic model for biofilm formation in a porous medium reactor, focusing on the impact of tangential diffusion of substrate and suspended bacteria on reactor performance. Comparing our model, which includes tangential diffusion, with a base model that omits it, reveals significant changes in system dynamics. Neglecting it can underestimate quenching length and biofilm accumulation downstream, even in advection-dominated regime. We also extend the study to a two-dimensional model, examining the effects of attachment and detachment of suspended bacteria. In the one-dimensional model, attachment strongly influences substrate depletion, especially with increased pore-space diffusion. The two-dimensional model highlights the importance of incorporating suspended bacteria and their processes for accurately describing biofilm formation, particularly in clean bed simulations. This research underscores the need for tangential diffusion in one-dimensional models and precise biomass exchange mechanisms in all cases.
author: Bottomley, Emma.

title: Mutual Aid and Third Places during the COVID-19 Pandemic: Intersections in Nova Scotia & New Brunswick.
abstract: Rural and peripheral to the economic core of Canada, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick, like much of Atlantic Canada, have been historically illustrated as economically under-developed provinces. While policy frameworks address many regional problems, Nova Scotians and New Brunswickers continue to come together in creative and innovative ways to respond to many of their needs, both at home and in their larger communities. Relying heavily on gray literature, this research explores how Nova Scotians and New Brunswickers experience 'Mutual Aid'. This research illustrates the unique landscape of Mutual Aid in the region and its connection to place. It will also explore how these geographies have shifted in the light of the COVID-19 pandemic. Finally, by revealing gaps in our current economic system, this research attempts to inform the way we think about community connectedness and welfare.
author: Dale, John Cameron.

title: Mutually Unbiased Bases and Weyl Commutation Relations in Quantum State Distinguishability.
abstract: This thesis explores the interplay between mutually unbiased bases, the Weyl commutation relation, and one-way local operations and classical communication (LOCC) distinguishability within quantum systems. We focus on three main concepts in this thesis: the Weyl commutation relation of matrices, the generalized Pauli matrices and mutually unbiased bases. We detail the well-known proof that in any dimension d, the number of mutually unbiased bases is at most d + 1. Specifically, we look at a proof that uses the argument of rank, as well as provide our own proof, which uses the argument of dimension. Leveraging these concepts, we develop an approach for distinguishing quantum states through one-way LOCC. Furthermore, we delve into established results related to common unbiased bases, which provide a framework for one-way LOCC distinguishability. The results and exposition contribute to a deeper understanding of quantum systems, with potential applications in secure communication protocols and quantum privacy.
author: Taank, Mukesh.

title: My Imaginary Outgroup Member Who Promotes Multiculturalism: The Role of Multiculturalism-Supportive Imagined Contact in Collective Action Tendencies, Outgroup Attitudes, and Acculturation Orientations in Canada and Turkey.
abstract: Imagined contact (mentally visualizing pleasant contact with the outgroup) has been shown as an effective strategy to improve intergroup relations (Crisp & Turner, 2009). For the first time, the current dissertation incorporated 'support for multiculturalism' into the imagined contact paradigm to investigate its potential as a tool to promote both positive intergroup relations and collective action intentions for greater social inequality. Multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact refers to imagining pleasant contact with an outgroup member that explicitly supports multiculturalism. The current dissertation examined the effectiveness of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact (relative to standard imagined contact and control conditions) on promoting positive acculturation orientations, collective action tendencies for greater social equality, and positive outgroup attitudes among minority and majority religious groups recruited from two different socio-cultural contexts, namely Canada and Turkey. Study 1 examined the effects of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact among Christian majority-group members in Canada. Findings indicated that multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact (relative to the control) improved outgroup attitudes toward Muslims and facilitated endorsement of transformation-integration toward Muslims, a welcoming acculturation orientation. Additionally, it mobilized Christian participants to engage in collective actions for greater equality of Muslims through increasing allyship identification with Muslims. Study 2 tested the effects of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact among Muslim minority-group members in Canada, and the findings demonstrated the ineffectiveness of this strategy among Muslims. Study 3 focused on the effects of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact among Sunni majority-group members in Turkey. Results showed the beneficial effects of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact on improving outgroup attitudes and promoting welcoming acculturation orientations only among participants who perceived the imagined task as positive. Study 4 explored the effects of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact among Alevi minority-group members in Turkey. Findings demonstrated the effectiveness of this strategy in promoting integration. Furthermore, findings indicated that multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact did not demobilize Alevis' willingness to engage in collective action tendencies on behalf of their ingroup. The current set of studies provides the first empirical evidence for the potential of multiculturalism-supportive imagined contact and has implications for imagined contact-based interventions and policies supporting intercultural engagement for promoting harmonious intergroup relations in multicultural societies.
author: Ray Yol, Elcin.

title: Navigating turbulence: the effects of eddy size on the swimming performance of walleye (Sander vitreus) larvae.
abstract: The larval stage of walleye represents a critical period determining population abundance and distribution. We evaluated potential larval recruitment mechanisms in a recirculating flow chamber, assessing critical swimming speeds and feeding rates in response to turbulence. Experiments with varying turbulent eddy sizes revealed that eddies two-thirds of larval size impeded swimming. Nonetheless, larvae were able to swim against turbulent flows. Feeding was first detected in their third week of development, coinciding with increased swimming speeds and access to inertial flow regimes. Larvae likely rely on strong currents for transport until they become stronger swimmers; and weak larval swimming abilities likely limit feeding. Unfavourable turbulent conditions in nature could impede the growth and survival of larval walleye, as swimming is crucial for dispersal, hunting, and evading predators. This study informs walleye conservation by providing swimming parameters necessary for realistic larval drift models and guiding stocking decisions to optimize recruitment success.
author: Chong, Keoni.

title: Neosporosis in Ontario broodmares: Seroprevalence and Risk Factors for Exposure to Neospora caninum and Neospora hughesi.
abstract: The seroprevalence and risk factors for exposure to Neospora caninum and Neospora hughesi in Ontario broodmares were investigated. The prevalence estimates for N. caninum and N. hughesi were 27.4% (60/219) and 29.7% (65/219), respectively. The IFAT cut-off titre for N. caninum and N. hughesi was 1:40 and 1:160, respectively. Risk factors for N. caninum included presence of farm dogs (OR = 6.70; 95% CI = 2.14 - 20.97; p = 0.001), and high stocking density (OR = 2.83; 95% CI = 1.27 - 6.30; p = 0.011). Presence of livestock, excluding cattle, was associated with reduced risk of exposure (OR = 0.17; 95% CI = 0.05 - 0.53; p = 0.002). The only risk factor for exposure to N. hughesi was feeding hay on the ground in the paddock (OR = 4.31; 95% CI = 1.65 - 11.22; p = 0.003). This study demonstrated significant exposure to Neospora spp. in Ontario broodmares.
author: Johns, Olivia.

title: Nested Association Mapping of Yield, Agronomic and Seed Composition Traits in a Canadian Elite x Exotic Soybean NAM Population.
abstract: The global demand for soybeans has emphasized enhancing yield and seed quality. In Canada, production has seen substantial growth, yet the genetic gains in yield are limited by the narrow genetic base within the existing germplasm. Addressing genetic diversity will be crucial for long term sustainability of genetic gain. This thesis explores the potential of exotic soybean germplasm to enhance yield, agronomic and seed quality traits in Canadian soybean breeding programs by crossing a high-yielding, elite Canadian cultivar (OAC Bruton) with four diverse, high-yielding exotic lines. The trait profiles of resulting RIL populations were characterized and a genome wide association study (GWAS) was conducted to dissect the genetic architecture of key traits and identify genomic regions from exotic sources that increase yield. The results demonstrate the utility of exotic germplasm for yield improvement of a Canadian cultivar and provide valuable information and breeding material for yield improvement in Canadian food-grade soybean.
author: Fortune, Katherine.

title: Neurobiology of Memory Modulation by Unconditioned and Conditioned Opioid Withdrawal.
abstract: There is evidence that unconditioned and conditioned opioid withdrawal modulate memory consolidation, and the experiments in this dissertation were designed to test the hypothesis that these states do so via shared neural mechanisms. Using the post-training method, in male Sprague-Dawley rats, it was first ascertained whether stress neurochemicals including noradrenaline (NA) and corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) are involved in modulation of object memory consolidation by unconditioned and conditioned heroin withdrawal. Next, it was explored if antagonism of CRF1 receptors within the central amygdala (CeA) mediate the memory-enhancing functions of unconditioned and conditioned heroin withdrawal. Finally, an immunohistochemical study investigated if unconditioned and conditioned heroin withdrawal induces comparable patterns of c-Fos expression in the extended amygdala and other anatomically and functionally linked regions. It was found that: i) Systemic blockade of NA and CRF neurotransmission interfered with the enhancement of object memory by both unconditioned and conditioned heroin withdrawal. ii) Antagonism of CeA-CRF1 receptors blocked enhancement of object memory by both unconditioned and conditioned withdrawal. iii) Unconditioned heroin withdrawal involved a set of regions linked to the bed nucleus of the stria terminalis, whereas the state of conditioned withdrawal was characterized by a pattern of c-Fos expression that involved the basolateral amygdala. In conclusion, these novel findings suggest that while pharmacological and psychological withdrawal states contribute to the strengthening of memory via common neural mechanisms involving NA neurotransmission and CRF within the CeA, the patterns of neuronal activation that characterize these states appear dissociable.
author: Baidoo, Nana.

title: New Approaches to Describe and Measure Wildlife Health in Ontario.
abstract: As emerging issues such as climate change and human population growth result in new health threats, it is important we learn how to build healthy, more resilient communities. To achieve this goal, a better understanding of the connections between wildlife and human health is needed. The objective of this project was to develop indicators capable of describing how wildlife health varies across Ontario. Data sources were identified, and indicators developed for 9 wildlife species. Developed indicators included the coefficient of variation and slope of the number of harvests per active hunter, the number of wildlife observations/100,00 people, and the percentage of dead or diseased wildlife observations. These indicators were summarized to generate species-specific and multi-species composite health scores, which were then mapped by wildlife management unit. These results provide a basis for future research to explore shared health determinants and to help target areas in need of better wildlife management.
author: Bayley, Daniel.

title: Nietzsche, Deleuze, and the Forces of Nihilism.
abstract: A central aspect of the Nietzschean philosophical heritage is the problem of nihilism as well as a continued engagement with whether we should pursue its definitive overcoming. Gilles Deleuze emerges as one of the foremost advocates for a successful overcoming in his work Nietzsche and Philosophy. My provisional aim in this dissertation is to critically evaluate whether Deleuze's claim of a definitive overcoming of nihilism is successful. Deleuze's success will depend on whether the negative forms of thinking that give rise to nihilism can be countered with a philosophy of affirmative difference that moves beyond a dialectics of negation. I begin by arguing that Deleuze fails to secure a definitive overcoming of nihilism but that his failure was nonetheless fruitful in supplying new conceptual tools to analyze nihilistic phenomena. Specifically, Deleuze's systemization of Nietzschean forces provides a theoretical framework from which it is possible to examine diverse forms of nihilism that emerge in disparate philosophical domains including semiotics, politics, and the metaphysics of becoming. The remainder of my dissertation will be experimental and constructive. I move the discussion of nihilism from Deleuze's early work on Nietzsche to his later collaboration with Félix Guattari in A Thousand Plateaus. I will argue that, despite a lack of explicit concern with the problem of nihilism in their coauthored work, Deleuze and Guattari remain engaged with identifying nihilistic tendencies that inhabit our semiotic systems, our notions of desire, our political formations, and our philosophical concepts like 'becoming.' I will apply Deleuze's logic of forces to analyze aspects of these different subjects to show how the effects of nihilism continue to unfold in our forms of thinking. I conclude by elaborating Deleuze and Guattari's notion of 'affective becoming,' which I assemble from a variety of concepts disseminated in Nietzsche and Philosophy and A Thousand Plateaus. I submit that their conceptualization of affective becoming, while in itself is not sufficient to definitively overcome nihilism, offers a convincing way to begin thinking beyond negative and nihilistic forms of thought.
author: Johnston, Kyle.

title: Nitrification Pathways in Membrane Aerated Biofilm Reactors to Treat Municipal Wastewater.
abstract: Recently, nitritation-denitritation has been demonstrated as an effective nitrogen removal alternative by reducing oxygen consumption and carbon supply, lowering CO2 emission, decreasing sludge production, and minimising the volume of reactors as compared to conventional nitrification and denitrification process. In the meantime, membrane aerated biofilm reactor (MABR) process becomes increasingly attractive due to the efficient dissolution of oxygen via molecular diffusion into water, resulting in up to five times more energy efficient than conventional aerators and above 75% of oxygen transfer efficiencies. Further potential benefits could be realized by combining partial nitrification with MABR. However, the relative importance of nitrogen removal pathways in MABR is still not well understood, due to the lack of reliable methods to measure the amount of their oxygen consumptions.Three MABR parallel testing systems equipped with ZeelungTM (SUEZ WTS, Hungry) membrane fibres were continuously operated for 230 days to treat synthetic wastewater. They included five sequential stages: biofilm seeding followed by the effects of substrate loadings in hybrid and non-hybrid operating modes, and C/N ratios in hybrid and non-hybrid operating modes. A mathematical MABR model based on wastewater process simulation software GPS-x® and calibrated with experimental data.To determine oxygen fluxes and mass transfer coefficients, three different methods were carried out. Results showed that gas phase mass balance (GPMB) method can be used to determine the oxygen flux reliably for MABRs used in wastewater treatment. Coupling with the liquid phase mass balance (LPMB) approach, the extent of partial nitrification can be estimated. Pressure decay mass transfer (PDMT) method could be applied to determine both mass transfer coefficient and the biofilm thickness. Hybrid MABRs (HMABRs) and MABRs achieved the COD removal of 91%, 87% and TN removal of 57% and 40%, respectively. Up to 14% and 11% of influent NH4+-N could be oxidized by partial nitrification by HMABRs and MABRs, respectively. Partial nitrification by both HMABRs and MABRs increased with the ammonia loading, and C/N ratio, while HMABRs had significant advantages in nitrogen pollutant removal at high substrate loading rates over MABRs. Further characterization of biofilm and suspended solids in HMABRs using fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) coupled with confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) showed that the biovolume fractions of nitrite-reducing bacteria were up to 26% and 5%, respectively. The oxygen half saturation constants for AOB and NOB were 0.14 and 0.82 gO2/m3, respectively, after model calibration using GPS-x®. This indicated that AOB can grow in lower dissolved oxygen concentration than NOB.
author: Qin, Na.

title: Nitrogen Oxide (NOx) Ambient Air Pollution; an Exploration of Sustainability and Health Impacts, and Design, Development and Validation of Photocatalytic Controls.
abstract: Ambient air pollution is a worldwide problem, on a scale such that air pollution is now a leading cause of human morbidity and mortality. Pollution is not limited to the outdoor environment, but infiltrates inside impacting the air where people live and work. This problem provides the starting point for the research presented herein: to assess knowledge gaps, investigate existing technology, and develop a novel air pollution control technology to reduce gaseous pollutant concentrations. One gaseous pollutant, nitrogen oxides (NOx), is selected as a focal point. To establish its merit, this research examined NOx's influence on sustainability and human health. Findings establish NOx as an encompassing indicator for global sustainability and a metric for gauging progress towards decarbonization. Additionally, a review of health literature indicated an astounding number and variety of negative health impacts resulting from ambient NOx exposure, with risks of mental health effects exceeding physical impacts. The identification of environmental and human health implications of ambient NOx clearly establishes a need to reduce ambient levels worldwide. Due to the de-localized nature of NOx sources, primarily traffic, outdoor mitigation of NOx emissions is not feasible, and attention is then shifted to indoor removal strategies. Indoor air quality can be improved through numerous avenues, but no current methods or technologies are capable of removing ambient NOx in a safe, sustainable fashion. Photocatalytic removal is potentially effective in removing NOx, and a patented photocatalyst offers solutions to the barriers that have prevented effective NOx removal with existing technologies. A design process was undertaken to develop a Gaseous Pollution Control Device (GPCD) prototype that was rigorously tested in the lab and showed 60-85% removal of nitric oxide (NO) and 50-100% removal of selected volatile organic compounds (VOCs). The GPCD was then industrially manufactured, and the commercial prototype was validated in pilot tests that confirmed the efficacy of the GPCD in removing ambient NOx, and other VOC pollutants. Implementing the GPCD for indoor ambient air purification will safely and effectively reduce ambient NOx and VOC concentrations and enhance health for individuals in the local environment.
author: Roth, Stephanie.

title: Norm Emergence Theory : An explication and empirical validation thereof.
abstract: The present dissertation overviews a novel approach to understanding the norm context guiding the behaviours of individuals, Norm Emergence Theory. This approach suggests that people can be categorized based on their elicited, pre-existing norm perceptions into perceivers of the Early Minority, Minority, Majority and Late Majority norm stages to understand the norm context guiding the behaviours of individuals. The dissertation examines the distinctions and also explores differences between these groupings in terms of measures of changes in norm perceptions and behavioural intentions. One pilot study and two survey-based quasi-experimental studies were run using university undergraduate participants (N = 644; N = 370). Results showed promising preliminary evidence for the norm stages as outlined under the proposed novel Norm Emergence Theory. Norm perceptions for both descriptive and injunctive results were shifted after exposure to a norm-based message, but differentially across the proposed norm stages, in that effects were predominantly found for early norm stages. The perceived norm stages also showed significant between-conditions differences in behavioural intentions, corresponding to a stepwise increase in the outcome variable. Correlational analyses further demonstrated a pattern of differing effects. Participants perceiving early norm stages showed stronger associations with behavioural intentions than participants perceiving later norm stages. Overall, differences in outcomes depended on a pre-existing elicited norm perception, meaning that regardless of the frame and content of norm-based messaging, these types of norm perceptions need to be considered in experimental manipulations of norms and in applied norm-based intervention campaigns. The dissertation helps address theoretical and applied-practical issues affecting the design of norm-based interventions.
author: Maurice, Gillian.

title: Norms in Ambiguous Situations: Scale Development and Impact on Norm Adherence.
abstract: Ambiguous situations increase the influence of norms on cognitions and behaviours. While this assumption seems popular in the literature, even a basic conceptual consensus on what constitutes an ambiguous situation appears to be missing. Our review suggested that 'ambiguous situations' were associated with a) the interpretation of a situation, b) normlessness, and c) norm conflict. In Study 1, we propose a novel scale - the Measure of Situational Ambiguity (MSA) - developed to quantify the amount of normative ambiguity in a situation. Participants use the scale to assess how interpretable a situation is and how many norms are identifiable (none or two). Situations are presented as vignettes where a behaviour is performed. In Study 1, participants (N = 330) used the MSA to rate five situations: studying, procrastinating, alcohol consumption, hot-water laundry, and coffee-cup disposal. The measure showed a two-factor structure (normlessness and norm conflict) rather than the anticipated three. An analysis of the correlations between the MSA and other measures revealed that normlessness was linked to greater uncertainty about social identities, higher self-control, less individualistic values, lower confidence in responses, and reduced engagement in the behaviour. Measuring norm conflict faced difficulties, with mostly non-significant correlations. In Study 2, we conducted an experiment in which participants inspected a series of graphs reporting statistics about opinions on various subjects (N = 590). Some of those graphs were manipulated to create the perception of a moderate norm, a strong norm, and norm conflict. Scores on the two subscales were used to moderate main effects. We found significant differences in norm perception for four out of five scenarios (except procrastination). Effect sizes are consistent with previous studies. No differences were found in MSA scores or past behaviour. We also identified an increase in intentions to study and a decrease in intentions to drink. No moderation effects of the MSA were identified for most scenarios. In general, support for the scale was limited, with further refinements suggested. Future research should also investigate new behaviors and examine different norms.
author: Dos Santos Pereira, Jonathan Jones.

title: Not Just Another Saturday Night: Strategizing Safety with Non-Urban Women Experiencing Intimate Partner Violence in Ontario.
abstract: Women living in non-urban communities are a high-risk population for intimate partner homicide (Dawson et al., 2019; Northcott, 2011). However, studies that have examined strategies women employed to respond to intimate partner violence (IPV) and safety planning are limited and urban-centric (Doherty, 2017). This dissertation answers four research questions related to increasing safety for women experiencing IPV in non-urban communities: (1) How do women living in non-urban communities experiencing IPV conceptualize safety? (2) How do women living in non-urban communities protect themselves from violence perpetrated by an intimate partner? (3) What challenges do service providers encounter when developing safety plans with women living in non-urban communities experiencing IPV?; and (4) How do women and service providers' perspectives align on how to increase safety in non-urban communities? This dissertation embraced methodological pluralism, drawing from social ecology, intersectionality and emotional geography to investigate women's perceptions of fear and safety, strategies women employed to be safer and challenges service providers encountered developing safety plans. In-depth interviews with 20 women who were living in a non-urban community and experienced IPV revealed that safety is a holistic and multidimensional concept, encompassing spatial, mental, physical, social, and cultural dimensions, which addresses the first research question. To answer the second research question, women primarily deflected physical danger and relied on private strategies (i.e., placating, resistance) to protect themselves and their child(ren). Women's responses were influenced by the geographic environment and their social positioning. Based on eight in-depth interviews with service providers, the inadequate police response and meeting women's basic needs (e.g., housing, transportation, groceries) were among the greatest challenges when safety planning, which addresses the third research question. To answer the fourth research question, a rural-specific poverty strategy and increased funding for social, emergency, and transportation services are needed to protect women from abusive men in non-urban communities. Overall, this dissertation demonstrates that place matters (Pruitt, 2008) for understanding women's experiences of IPV and increasing their safety. Therefore, policies and initiatives aimed at reducing gender-based violence in Canada must consider the geographic context in which women are living to overcome postcode (in)justice (George & Harris, 2014).
author: Bader, Danielle.

title: Novel Approaches to Attenuate Acute OLZ-Induce Hyperglycemia.
abstract: OLZ (OLZ), a second-generation antipsychotic (SGA) is highly prescribed for patients suffering from schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and other 'off-label' conditions such as depression, and attention deficit disorder. There are a plethora of studies demonstrating that acute OLZ treatment causes rapid episodes of hyperglycemia. As repeated hyperglycemic episodes are a risk factor for the development of cardiovascular diseases it is important to identify approaches to attenuate the OLZ-induced excursions in blood glucose. The work presented in this thesis investigated the effects of physical activity, 5'AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) activation, fasting and ketogenic diets in protecting against acute OLZ induced hyperglycemia. In study one I investigated if an overnight bout of voluntary wheel running (VWR), was sufficient to protect against acute OLZ-induced excursions in blood glucose. I found that a single session of VWR attenuated OLZ induced increases in blood glucose in parallel with blunted increases in glucagon and this effect was maintained in mice with an exaggerated blood glucose response to OLZ. The second study focused on the role of AMPK β1 containing complexes in modulating the acute blood glucose response to OLZ. I revealed that reductions in AMPK activity potentiate the effects of acute OLZ treatment on blood glucose, whereas specifically targeting AMPK β1 containing complexes pharmacologically is sufficient to protect against OLZ-induced hyperglycemia.In the third study I examined if interventions which increase circulating ketone bodies (fasting, beta-hydroxybutyrate, ketone esters or a ketogenic diet (KD)) would be sufficient to protect against the acute metabolic side effects of OLZ. I demonstrated that fasting and the short-term consumption of a KD protected against acute antipsychotic (AP)-induced perturbations in glucose homeostasis, whereas manipulations which acutely increase circulating ketone bodies do not elicit the same beneficial effects.The studies in this thesis are the first to demonstrate protective effects of voluntary physical activity, AMPK activation and increases in ketone bodies against OLZ-induced hyperglycemia. In all, this work contributes significantly to our understanding of different approaches to protect against acute OLZ-induced perturbations in glucose homeostasis and could be important in the design of novel co-treatment strategies to alleviate the metabolic side effects of APs.
author: Shamshoum, Hesham.

title: Novel Auxiliary Fixture System to Enhance Milled Thin-Walled Parts Quality.
abstract: Due to the high flexibility of thin-walled workpieces, vibrations are a common problem when milling thin-walled workpieces. Vibrations lead to poor surface finish and reduced machining efficiency. Employing multi-pin fixtures to increase the damping and stiffness of the vibration modes of the workpieces is one of the best methods to suppress vibrations. The performance of multi-pin fixtures depends on the efficiency of their pin's mechanism. In this study, a novel pin mechanism was developed based on the application of meteorological elastomer (MRE) Nine pins were used in building a new fixture. Hammer test results showed that the mechanism provided adjustable damping and stiffness, and with increasing the electric current, the damping and stiffness increased. Based on contact force measurements, it was found that the pin contact force is soft and can be adjusted. For the fixture with a single pin, results of hammer tests showed an increase in the dynamic properties of vibration modes of workpieces. The properties further increased with increased electrical current. For the single-pin fixtures, four locations (1, 2, 4 or 5) were tested. It was found that Location 5 had the best vibration suppression and the lowest surface roughness combined with the lowest cutting force value. Fixtures were also tested with more than one pin, which resulted in increased properties compared to fixtures with single pins. A multi-pocked industrial workpiece was milled in order to evaluate the performance of a six-pin fixture. The results showed that the stiffness increased by 90%, and the damping ratio was increased tenfold. The cutting force decreased by 70% for milling a slot in the bottom floor of the workpiece and by 40% for milling a sidewall of the workpiece. The surface roughness of the bottom floor decreased from 2.5 μm to about 0.4 μm. However, cutting the sidewall decreased the surface roughness from 2.3 to 0.3 μm. Here, we show that it is possible to design a fixture with adjustable dynamic properties. for milling thin-walled workpieces with complex geometric shapes. Due to the use of MR elastomer, this design is cost-effective, easy to assemble, and provides high vibration suppression performance.
author: Al Zubaidi, Nihad.

title: Novel Co-Treatment Strategies to Attenuate Acute Olanzapine-Induced Metabolic Dysfunction.
abstract: Olanzapine is a second-generation antipsychotic (SGA) that is commonly prescribed for the treatment of schizophrenia and several on- and off-label conditions. While effective in reducing psychoses, acute olanzapine treatment causes rapid hyperglycemia, insulin resistance, and dyslipidemia and these perturbations are linked to an increased risk of developing cardiometabolic disease. The aim of this thesis was to investigate novel pharmacological approaches with GLP1 and/or amylin analogs to offset the acute negative metabolic side effects of olanzapine.In study 1, it was found that co-treatment with glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP1) receptor agonists protect against olanzapine-induced hyperglycemia and lipidemia while these outcomes are worsened by GLP1 receptor antagonism. Study 2 investigated compounds which synergistically enhance endogenous GLP1 as a strategy to reduce the metabolic side effects of olanzapine. In this study it was found that raising endogenous GLP1 concentrations to pharmacological levels can attenuate olanzapine-induced perturbations in glucose and lipid metabolism under glucose stimulated conditions. Study 3 explored the efficacy of amylin analogs such as pramlintide, to combat the metabolic side effects of olanzapine and found that pramlintide tended to reduce olanzapine-induced hyperglycemia and enhanced the protective effects of low dose liraglutide, a GLP1 receptor agonist, against olanzapine-induced metabolic dysfunction. Together, the studies in this thesis highlight new benefits of common pharmacological treatments which leverage GLP1 and/or amylin signalling to exert protection against acute olanzapine-induced metabolic dysfunction and could be important in designing novel treatment strategies to alleviate side effects of antipsychotics.
author: Medak, Kyle D.

title: Novel Pipeline for Large-Scale Comparative Population Genetics.
abstract: This study determined population genetic structure measures, compared these measures across species with different biological traits; and created efficient, reproducible, reusable programming modules that are publicly available for future research. Cytochrome C Oxidase subunit I gene sequences from Diptera (true fly) species from Greenland and Canada were used as a case study and proof of concept. I hypothesized that population genetic structure measures will be influenced by the biological traits of organisms. Data were pulled from public databases, as well as taxon-specific literature. The R pipeline includes fifteen modules that can be adapted and applied to a diverse set of animal groups, geographic regions, genes, and traits. Habitat, larval diet, geographical distance, latitude, and longitude were all significantly related to population genetic structure in Diptera. Overall, this study has created efficient, reusable bioinformatics modules, as well as provided insight into the factors affecting population genetic structure in Northern fly communities.
author: Majoros, Samantha.

title: Novel Roles of Cardiac-derived Erythropoietin in Disease.
abstract: Myocardial infarction (MI) is a leading cause of death globally. Over 3 million individuals experience ST-segment elevation MI (STEMI) each year. Despite considerable research into cytoprotective mechanisms, no clinically available therapy has been developed that directly reduces ischemic cardiac injury. Understanding the pathophysiological mechanisms underlying MI should reveal novel strategies for treatment and diagnostic purposes (i.e., biomarkers). Emerging preclinical data shows erythropoietin (EPO), a canonically renal-derived erythropoietic cytokine, is also produced by the heart under hypoxic conditions in vitro and in vivo. However, whether these findings translate to functionally relevant outcomes post-MI is currently unknown. Therefore, the overarching purpose of this thesis was to define the contributions to cardioprotection by endogenously produced EPO in the heart and fundamentally improve our understanding of non-canonical EPO biology. My objective was to characterize the systemic (i.e., endocrine) and local (i.e., autocrine/paracrine) effects of cardiac EPO using two in vivo pre-clinical models (i.e., murine permanent occlusion MI and a cardiomyocyte-specific EPO knockout mouse). We hypothesized: 1) cardiac ischemia localized to the heart would induce cardiac Epo expression and contribute to erythropoiesis, and 2) loss of endogenous cardiomyocyte-derived Epo would increase myocardial susceptibility to ischemia-reperfusion injury and impair cardiac functional recovery. Here we show MI upregulates cardiac Epo, stimulating erythropoiesis, alongside the expression of a novel exon 4-deleted Epo splice variant (termed mV-Epo). Administration of recombinant human EPO (rhEPO) or a human orthologue to the mV-EPO variant (hV-EPO) was cardioprotective compared to vehicle-treated MI mice. Therefore, as a non-erythropoietic EPO isoform, hV-EPO represents a promising strategy for amplifying endogenous cardiac EPO signaling in post-MI patients without the rheological side effects posed by traditional rhEPOs. In our second model, cardiomyocyte-specific Epo deletion induced a compensatory increase in Epo expression by neighboring endothelial cells that enabled cellular hypertrophy and superior cardiac function, and reduced ischemia-reperfusion injury. Collectively, cardiac EPO has powerful physiological relevance as a cardioprotectant and erythropoietic cytokine. These findings fundamentally shift our understanding of EPO biology - confirming it as both an endocrine and autocrine/paracrine cardiac hormone - and reinvigorate its therapeutic potential for ameliorating MI-induced dysfunction.
author: Marrow, Jade P.

title: Novel Strategies for Sustainable Rapid Breeding of Cannabis sativa L.
abstract: Currently, feminized cannabis seeds are produced by spraying 'XX' plants with silver-based compounds to block ethylene receptors. 1-MCP provides a more environmentally benign alternative to silver. Exposure to 100µM gaseous 1-MCP for 72 hours resulted in a 30.4% conversion to male flowers compared to 72.6% for the 3mM STS (silver thiosulfate) treatments. The use of in-vitro plants to reduce generation time is also investigated. In-vitro cannabis explants treated with 10µM PBZ (paclobutrazol) had increased harvest index (HI) (30.8%) and floral weight (135mg/plant) compared to the control with 15.8% HI and 60mg floral weight. No treatment induced a more rapid transition to flowering compared to the control. Lastly, polyploidy is explored as a method of crop improvement. The tetraploids matured 20 days before the diploids and produced the same amount of floral material calculated as grams short day-1 with 3.43g day-1 in diploid plants and 3.62g day-1 for tetraploids.
author: Baiton, Austin.

title: Novel Treatment of Acute Myeloid Leukemia by Targeting Complex II in the Mitochondrial Electron Transport Chain.
abstract: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a hematological malignancy characterized by an increased reliance on mitochondria-related energetic pathways (e.g., oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS)); and consistently, complex II (CII) of the mitochondrial electron transport chain has elevated activity in AML, providing a potential therapeutic target. Despite this, direct CII inhibition is unreported in AML research, and the mechanisms that lead to divergent fates of AML and normal cells upon CII inhibition are unknown. Thus, the primary aim is to characterize the effects of direct CII inhibition on AML cells through genetic and pharmacological approaches. AML cell lines were genetically impaired using lentiviral-mediated knockdown, suppressing CII activity and delaying in vitro and in vivo leukemia cell growth. To confirm these effects pharmacologically, an in silico screen and molecular docking study identified shikonin, a plant-derived bioactive, to bind to CII. Shikonin inhibits CII activity and is selectively cytotoxic to AML bulk and CD34+ cells but not normal progenitors. The metabolic consequences of CII impairment, monitored through stable isotope tracing, suppressed the oxidative tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle. Notably, CII inhibition results in activation of reductive carboxylation of glutamine, an alternate metabolic pathway that regenerates TCA intermediates when OXPHOS is impaired. Here, loss of CII activity results in divergent cell fates whereby normal hematopoietic, but not AML cells, sufficiently used reductive carboxylation of glutamine to overcome TCA cycle truncation, sustain aspartate levels, and avert cell death. Furthermore, chronic exposure to shikonin leads to metabolic reprogramming that is more glycolytic and less mitochondrial. This work provides insight into CII inhibition in AML, exposing reductive carboxylation to support aspartate biosynthesis as a novel metabolic vulnerability in AML targeted by shikonin, a newly identified CII inhibitor.
author: Roma, Alessia.

title: Novel approaches for the Genetic Improvement of Fertility and Reproduction in Dairy Cattle.
abstract: For decades, animal breeding programs have allowed the dairy industry to select highly productive cows, however, at the same time, reproductive performance has declined. The challenge of selecting fertility-related traits is their low heritability and selection accuracy. Traits that are closer to the biology of the animal, combined with a better understanding of the biological mechanisms and genetic architecture of fertility, could enhance the efficiency of genetic selection.In the first study of this thesis, a recently developed indicator of pregnancy rate, called size and position score of the reproductive tract, was investigated as a potential novel trait for genetic selection to improve fertility. For this aim, genetic parameters were estimated to determine the potential of the score as a novel trait and how it genetically correlates with other economically important traits evaluated in Canada.More than just the conception ability of females, the reproductive outcome is also defined by the gametic compatibility of the mating pair. Little is known about pair compatibility in livestock and investigating its genetic background provides insights into its possible application to optimize mate allocation. In the second study of this thesis, transmission ratio distortion (TRD), a deviation from Mendelian inheritance expectations, was used to identify genomic regions associated with gametic incompatibility. Functional analyses of these regions were used to identify potential biological mechanisms involved, which is the first step towards developing applications for improved mate allocations.The TRD regions identified in the second study were observed to overlap with copy number variant (CNV) regions associated with stillbirth. These CNV are structural variants that can influence gene expression and structure, impacting phenotypic variance. As a result, CNV could be the origin of TRD signals. Considering causes of stillbirth are still largely unknown, functional analyses of these overlapping regions were carried out in the third study to provide insights into the mechanisms, which may be underlying stillbirth in dairy cows. Results pinpointed calving difficulty and, more interestingly, an abnormal uterine inflammation process as potential causes of stillbirth.
author: Martin, Audrey A.A.

title: Numerical and Experimental Assessment of Nano MQL Machining of Ti-6Al-4V.
abstract: Nano Minimum Quantity Lubrication (NFMQL) is an environmentally sustainable strategy that provides benefits similar to conventional cooling strategies. The literature provides sufficient experimental studies to attest the benefits of NFMQL machining; however, numerical modeling of NFMQL machining has not received significant attention. Since machining with coolant is a Multiphysics phenomenon, Finite Element Method (FEM) and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) are needed to construct a dependable numerical model. The overall objective of this study was to develop a FEM-CFD coupled numerical model that could reliably predict cutting force and cutting temperature in the NFMQL machining of Ti-6Al-4v. Additionally, multiphase CFD models for NFMQL flow simulation and FEM simulations to capture interface friction in NFMQL machining were also investigated. Orthogonal machining experiments of Ti-6Al-4v were conducted to obtain cutting force and cutting temperature data for dry, NFMQL, Minimum Quantity Lubrication (MQL), and flood cooling strategies. The experimental data was used to validate the numerical model developed for NFMQL machining. Further, experiments were conducted to calculate the heat transfer coefficient (h) for NFMQL flow and to calculate coefficient of friction value (µ) in a NFMQL machining process. A multiphase CFD model developed for NFMQL flow predicted the heat transfer coefficient with an average error of 13%. A novel FEM simulation to capture the effect of rolling nanoparticles on cutting force was also presented. A FEM-CFD sequential model and a coupled model were developed to simulate the NFMQL machining process. The coupled model predicted the cutting force with an average error of 11.27% while the sequential model had an average error of 9.16%. The average error in temperature prediction was 11.50% for the coupled model and 9.61% for the sequential model. The error of the coupled model was higher than the sequential model since a constant film coefficient value was utilized for elements with different temperatures along the boundary film. However, both models provide better accuracy than other numerical models available in literature. The coupled model framework developed can be applied to any cooling strategy and is the first step towards achieving high level coupling between FEM and CFD codes for a machining simulation.
author: Nouzil, Ibrahim.

title: Nutrient Addition does not Allow for Selection for Extended Floral Longevity in Response to Simulated Pollinator Decline in Lobelia siphilitica.
abstract: Plants can respond to pollinator decline by extending how long their flowers are open and receptive to pollination (i.e., floral longevity). To test whether selection for extended floral longevity in response to pollinator decline depends on abiotic resource availability, I measured floral longevity and seeds per fruit of L. siphilitica plants in experimentally manipulated pollination and soil nutrient treatment combinations. I found that increased soil nutrients had no immediate effect on floral longevity, while experimentally simulated pollinator decline extended longevity. However, neither increased soil nutrients nor simulated pollinator decline affected selection for extended floral longevity. Instead, there was no evidence of selection on longevity in any treatment combination. These results indicate that extended floral longevity will not help plants maintain seed production as pollinators decline and suggests that they will need to evolve other traits that increase pollinator attraction to persist in environments with fewer pollinators.
author: Whitehead, Amanda.

title: Object-feature reinstatement into working memory? Separating the direct and indirect effect of long-term memory on attentional selection.
abstract: Attentional templates guide attention towards stimuli that match the features of an item being searched for. Research has shown that prior knowledge of an item in long-term memory (LTM) can indirectly become integrated into a visual working memory (VWM) based template and bias attention. On the other hand, it has also been shown that these templates can be stored in LTM and directly guide attention. To further understand how LTM guides attentional selection, in the present study participants used LTM to learn a set of objects with specific colours and then searched for the objects amongst new or old distractors. We found that for VWM-based templates, RTs were significantly affected by the previously learned colour association, suggesting that LTM indirectly tunes the attentional template in VWM. Instead, when search is guided directly by LTM, the effects on search time are likely related to a post-perceptual process rather than attention.
author: Kespe, Jessica.

title: On Rice and Weddings: An Investigation of the Impacts of Indonesia's Raskin Program on the Incidence of Child Marriage.
abstract: We investigate the role that food security plays in a family's decision to arrange early marriages for their daughters. Specifically, we study Indonesia's Raskin program, the world'slargest food assistance program. We implement household fixed effects to control for endogenous selection to the Raskin program, allowing us to compare siblings by isolating thewithin-household variation in the exposure to the program. When controlling for household-level selection to the program, the Raskin program had no significant impact on the incidenceof child marriage. We investigate potential mechanisms behind this relationship by exploringthe effect of premarital transactions, religion, and cultural institutions.
author: Lorenz, Aaron.

title: One Shell to Rule Them All? Evaluating Hydrodynamic Trade-Offs Due to Positioning in Freshwater Mussels.
abstract: Unionid mussels are important in freshwaters because of their roles as ecosystem engineers (e.g., water quality and nutrient regulation). Water flow plays a significant role in the life of mussels, facilitating several processes including reproduction and food intake. Excessive hydrodynamic forces, however, are also responsible for the dislodgement of mussels, especially when mussels engage in feeding and reproduction and are exposed above the sediment-water interface. This research presents both field and computational fluid dynamics (CFD) modeling to understand the advantages and disadvantages (i.e., trade-offs) of mussel orientation at different water speed. Freshwater mussels were collected from rivers in Southern Ontario, and the species and orientation, relative to flow in the river, was recorded. For CFD modeling, COMSOL Multiphysics software was used to model these interactions with Lampsilis siliquoidea. Higher angles of attack and vertical angles have higher drag coefficients. The significance of this research is to provide a broader understanding of the impact of hydrodynamic forces on living organisms in moving fluids. In addition, the evolutionary history of freshwater mussels will be better understood relative to water flow characteristics.
author: Sabeti-Mehr, Emile.

title: Ontological Commitments in Scientific Explanation.
abstract: Explanation is an essential aim of science, and the dominant approach to scientific explanation is causal. According to causal accounts, scientific theories explain phenomena by appealing to causes or causal mechanisms. Wesley Salmon and James Woodward defend a causal approach to scientific explanation in the two main but different causal approaches in the literature. These two approaches clearly introduce and defend ontological commitments in a scientific theory of explanation. However, since the mid-2000s, there has been a defence of non-causal approaches that we call the new generation of defenders of non-causal explanations (compared to Hempel, Kitcher and their followers). This new generation introduces examples in different scientific fields like physics, mathematics, biology, and neuroscience, where causal explanations do not work. They argue for the need for non-causal explanations in these cases. However, this new generation is silent about the ontological implications of non-causal explanations. We argue that even these non-causal approaches have ontological commitments to weakly emergent properties and entities. To defend non-causal approaches to explanation, this new generation appeals to the role of scientific models in scientific explanation. They follow Ronald Giere's approach to scientific models, according to which scientific theories represent the target systems through scientific models, not mere physical laws. Some philosophers, Like Alisa Bokulich, go further than Giere and argue that, in some important cases, 'fictional models' have explanatory power. She refers to semiclassical mechanics to defend these fictional models' explanatory power. We agree with the unavoidable role of non-causal accounts in scientific explanations. Also, we agree with Bokulich to defend the explanatory power of these highly idealized models in science. However, contrary to Bokulich, we argue that these semiclassical models include weakly emergent properties that give them explanatory power. To this aim, we introduce and defend weak emergentism and its two main characteristics. Then we show that these highly idealized models satisfy these two conditions and have weakly emergent properties. Finally, we use this approach in three more examples (damped oscillators, universal macro-behaviours, and rainbows) to show that even non-causal approaches to explanation have ontological commitments to weakly emergent properties.
author: Moazzenzadeh, Jahangir.

title: Open-source Tools to Facilitate Preclinical Alcohol and Nicotine Use Research.
abstract: As the cost of state-of-the-art scientific equipment is rising, the research funding in many countries falling or stagnating, and the majority of funding still concentrating in few wealthy institutions, the need for alternatives to prohibitively expensive commercial equipment is becoming a necessity for new and underfunded labs to survive. Additionally, many researchers are held hostage by their equipment, without the ability to adapt their methodology to answer the questions they are interested in. This leads to research driven by available technology, which is problematic if the only equipment available is that which is common enough to be commercially viable. Low-cost, open-source hardware and software not only offer a unique opportunity to promote equity in the global scientific community, but also allow for question specific, research-driven technological advancement in niche domains. The following thesis presents the design, construction, and operation of three original open-source projects and the research findings made possible by their conception. These projects are aimed at improving the translational validity of findings from preclinical models of alcohol and nicotine use. Amid a scarcity of effective treatment options for substance use disorders, there has recently been increased efforts to improve translational validity in preclinical addiction research. Alcohol and nicotine use represent two of the most significant global health concerns, and, exacerbated by the changing landscape of youth nicotine use by vaping, provide unique health concerns for vulnerable populations. Each project presented provides a unique area of translational improvement. The first project, an automated two-bottle choice test, improves the quality of tracking alcohol consumption in rats by adding measurement of drinking microstructure and time stamping drinking bouts for circadian analysis. The second project, FARESHARE, uses radio frequency identification technology to enable the study of alcohol drinking in social contexts. The third project, OpenVape, allows for the passive exposure of animals to commonly used commercial e-cigarette vapour, opening up an affordable opportunity for researchers to explore the short- and long-term health consequences of vaping.
author: Frie, Jude.

title: Optical Conductivity of a Three-dimensional Semi-Dirac Model.
abstract: With the discovery of graphene in 2004, experiments have quickly confirmed that many of its low energy properties are well-described by a Dirac Hamiltonian for massless fermions. This has created intense interest in other Dirac-like materials and model Hamiltonians. In this thesis, we compute the electronic density of states and optical conductivity for a semi-Dirac model in three dimensions. This model is Dirac-like in two of the dimensions and free-electron-like in the third, generalizing the two-dimensional semi-Dirac Hamiltonian to three dimensions. We find different power-law behaviour in the frequency-dependent conductivity in different directions, which would provide a signature of the Hamiltonian if seen in experiments. We also provide an analytic form for the conductivity in a more general case and contrast our results with other models in the literature, for example, graphene, three-dimensional Weyl, and the two-dimensional semi-Dirac.
author: Marques, Alexandre.

title: Optimization of Tolpyralate for Weed Control in Corn (Zea mays L.).
abstract: Thirty field experiments were conducted from 2019-2021 at six locations in Ontario, Canada to optimize the efficacy of tolpyralate for weed control in corn. Two studies investigated the interaction between 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (HPPD)-inhibitors (tolpyralate, mesotrione, and topramezone) and reactive oxygen species (ROS)-generators (atrazine, bromoxynil, bentazon, and glufosinate) on annual weed species and one study focused on the interaction on glyphosate-resistant Canada fleabane. Co-application of HPPD-inhibitors and ROS-generators were generally synergistic or additive for weed control, but the response depended on the herbicide, rate of herbicide, weed species, and response parameter. In contrast to mesotrione, tolpyralate was antagonistic with glufosinate for common ragweed, Setaria spp., and barnyardgrass control at 8 weeks after application. The effective dose of atrazine to complement tolpyralate was established for seven annual weed species. Three separate studies identified that MSO Concentrate®, Merge®, or Carrier® adjuvants should be added to tolpyralate, tolpyralate + atrazine, or tolpyralate + bromoxynil.
author: Fluttert, John.

title: Optimization of Weed Control in E3 Soybean [Glycine max (L.) Merr.].
abstract: Twenty field experiments were conducted from 2021-2022 at nine locations in Ontario, Canada to optimize weed control in E3 soybean. Two studies compared one- and two-pass weed control programs for control of glyphosate-resistant (GR) Canada fleabane and multiple herbicide-resistant (MHR) waterhemp. Two-pass weed control programs provided greater control of GR Canada fleabane and MHR waterhemp. Another study investigated herbicide interactions for volunteer corn control in E3 soybean. The co-application of 2,4-D choline or dicamba with quizalofop-p-ethyl or clethodim resulted in antagonistic herbicide interactions and reduced control. Glufosinate co-applied with quizalofop-p-ethyl or clethodim resulted in a synergistic interaction. The effect of glufosinate rate, the addition of AMS, and weed size at application was established for five annual weed species. The effect of glufosinate rate, the addition of AMS, and weed size at application is weed species specific. Generally, control is improved with increased rate and reduced weed size at application.
author: Duenk, Emily.

title: Optimizing Boron and Molybdenum Delivery for Contrasting Cultivars of Subirrigated, Greenhouse-grown Pot Chrysanthemums.
abstract: Greenhouse floriculture operations must produce high-yielding, quality flowers while minimizing environmental risk. I hypothesized that boron and molybdenum delivery to modern cultivars of subirrigated chrysanthemum can be optimized, compared to industry standards, while maintaining plant yield and quality. Two experiments were conducted for each micronutrient in a naturally-lit greenhouse using a randomized complete block split-plot design. Boron (5.00-0.313 μmol L-1) or molybdenum (0.50-0.031 μmol L-1), corresponding to 100-6.25% of current industry standards, was supplied in an otherwise balanced nutrient solution during vegetative growth only. Plant/flower morphology and development, visible symptoms of nutrient deficiency, and tissue nutrient levels indicated that boron and molybdenum supplies could be successfully reduced by 75-94% through improved nutrient use efficiency. This research contributes to the development of a sustainable low-input nutrient delivery strategy for floricultural operations, wherein nutrient supply is interrupted during reproductive growth and optimized during vegetative growth.
author: Teeter-Wood, Katherine.

title: Optimizing Community-Based Wildlife Monitoring: Prioritizing Indigenous Knowledge and Values.
abstract: Global recognition of the value of Indigenous stewardship has led to a growing interest in weaving together Indigenous and Western sciences to tackle current environmental challenges, including biodiversity loss. However, with a lack of practical examples of cross-cultural collaborations within ecology, it can be difficult to know how to accomplish this in practice. In partnership with Magnetawan First Nation, this thesis provides a case-study for respectful and meaningful collaboration between Indigenous and non-Indigenous partners within wildlife monitoring in two complimentary ways.
