In this thesis, I examine digital agriculture technologies such as e-commerce platforms, and alternative proteins such as cellular agriculture as two agri-food innovations with much promise to fundamentally transform the way we produce, process, distribute, and consume food across scales. I contribute to sustainability transformation literature and practice by undertaking empirically informed critiques of agri-food innovations, developing a more nuanced characterization for their potential promise, and advancing community-based research to scrutinize their impacts on-the-ground. My thesis begins by examining the sustainability-related tradeoffs of entomophagy and cellular agriculture. I found that alternative proteins have substantial potential to positively impact the global food system across multiple dimensions, such as food price stability and environmental sustainability; however, technical barriers to scale and high potential cost may dampen their impact. In my later chapters I assess the framings of and potential futures for agri-food innovation. I found three distinct framings for how digital agriculture technologies are transforming food systems, and a lack of civil society participation within these discussions. In addition, I found that access, integration, and centralization are key dimensions across which cellular agriculture can be assessed in terms of its promise to transform food systems. In my final phase of research, I partnered with an e-commerce platform to survey local food system stakeholders in Ontario to better understand their digital technology needs. I found that stakeholders are keen to use digital tools, but political economic barriers across the digital platform sector prevent their widespread benefits from being realized. This thesis advances sustainability transformation scholarship by explicitly considering its normative dimensions: transformation of what? For what purpose? By whom? Crucially, the myriad ways in which agri-food innovations are governed can result in diverse forms of change, following less or more business-as-usual processes. Hybridized governance processes and increased public and civil society participation are crucial to achieve sustainable food system transformations. Future scholarship that advances actionable research projects with civil society and public stakeholders can open space for imagined, desired, and possible futures.
author: Glaros, Alesandros.

title: Synergistic Interactions of Yellow Mustard Gum with Kappa-Carrageenan.
abstract: Yellow mustard gum (YMG) is a value-added ingredient derived from yellow mustard seed by-products. It exhibits shear-thinning flow behavior and excellent emulsifying and stabilizing abilities in both oil/water and water/oil systems. These properties remain relatively stable under various pH, temperature, and solute conditions. However, the current extraction technology, involving hot water soaking and ethanol precipitation, limits its commercial production and application. This thesis aimed to explore and demonstrate the commercial potential of YMG by investigating its synergistic gelation blend with κ-carrageenan. Rheology, calorimetry (DSC), and microscopy (Cryo-SEM) were employed to study YMG and its synergistic blends. A scale-up production protocol was developed, utilizing minimal processing steps and no organic solvents. The pilot-scale YMG product exhibited similar chemical composition and rheological properties to the purified YMG obtained through a previous lab-scale method. Moreover, this YMG product effectively encapsulated essential oil blends and provided long-term stability in an oil-in-water system. Synergistic interactions between YMG and κ-carrageenan were observed, differentiating them from the synergistic interaction of YMG with locust bean gum (LBG). YMG-κ-carrageenan (YMG-C) gels demonstrated strong and elastic properties, whereas YMG-LBG gels exhibited adhesive and paste-like behavior. Gelation of YMG-C was induced by cooling, similar to κ-carrageenan, while YMG-LBG gels remained stable throughout the gelation process, forming and stabilizing quickly after heating and mixing. The synergism between YMG and κ-carrageenan stemmed from the compatibility of the two gums, resulting in the formation of a single-phase cooperative gel when κ-carrageenan dominated the system (YMG:κ-carrageenan 3 to 7). The synergistic blend led to stronger gel strength primarily due to polymer interactions between the two gums. YMG did not affect the coil-to-helix transition of κ-carrageenan but participated in its helix aggregation during gel setting. These findings provide valuable insights to support the commercial production and application of YMG as a clean-label natural gum.
author: Wang, Xinya.

title: Synthesis and Electrochemical Study of Graphene-Based Nanomaterials for Energy Conversion and Storage.
abstract: The objective of this thesis is to develop three-dimensional (3D) graphene-based nanomaterials for energy applications. The research in this project involved three steps: understanding three-dimensional structures and their functionalization; controlling the pore structure for enhanced performance; and developing a simple synthesis method for 3D reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Hydrothermal N-doping on two-dimensional graphene oxide (GO) and 3D interconnected reduced graphene oxide (ICrGO) were investigated. The capacitance of N-doped ICrGO (N-ICrGO) was 262 Fg−1(at 2 A g-1), and the N-ICrGO symmetric capacitor retained 95% capacity after 2400 cycles. Furthermore, N-ICrGO catalyzed the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with a 0.91 V vs RHE onset potential, which was further enhanced through the decoration of Pd nanoparticles (Pd-N-ICrGO) to achieve 0.98 V vs RHE and a high methanol tolerance, in contrast to the Pt/C catalyst. It was demonstrated that the 3D structure coupled with N-doping enhanced the overall supercapacitor performance and ORR. A rapid microwave-hydrothermal (MH) technique was developed to produce rGO (M-rGO) without the use of reducing agents. The MH process was optimized in terms of temperature (220°C) and duration (5 min) to tune the structure and composition. The M-rGO exhibited higher capacitance of 298 Fg−1 (at 1 A g−1), as well as much better capacitance retention and cyclability in contrast to conventional chemically reduced graphene oxide (C-rGO) and thermally reduced graphene oxide (T-rGO). The superb energy storage performance of M-rGO could be attributed to its large surface area (315 m2 g-1), and uniform mesoporous structure. A one-pot improved synthesis was developed and optimized (oxidation duration, reduction temperature), which resulted in interconnected reduced graphene oxide (denoted as i-rGO). The optimized i-rGO demonstrated a high C/O ratio (5.2), nominal defect density (0.90), and a specific capacitance of 256 F g-1 (at 1 A g-1) in an aqueous electrolyte with an 84% contribution from electrical double layer capacitance. Further, the i-rGO symmetric capacitor demonstrated a energy density of 24.4 Wh kg-1; power density of 28 kW kg-1; and a capacity retention of 91% following 10000 cycles at 10 A g-1. This study articulates the facile synthesis of 3D i-rGO for efficient energy storage.
author: Thiruppathi, Antony Raj.

title: Synthesis and Investigation of Graphene-based Nanomaterials for Antimicrobial Applications.
abstract: With the global rise in infectious disease, the demand for novel antimicrobial materials has risen to match. Nanomaterials in particular offer a novel solution as their unique properties allows them to have great activity relative to their quantity. This thesis focuses on the use of graphene-based nanocomposites as viable antimicrobial nanomaterials. Initially, a literature review is done to highlight the recent advances in the field and discuss avenues that can be taken. The first project focuses on silver-loaded graphene-based materials. Antibacterial activity of the silver materials against E. coli reached up to 93.4%, and up to 97% and 70% for S. enterica and S. aures, respectively. Using thiocoumarin silver sensor 1 (TcAg1) dye to investigate the mechanism of activity, it was concluded that the mechanism of activity of all the materials investigated in this thesis is the release of silver ions that would then attack the cellular structure of the bacteria. In the second project, bimetallic silver copper nanocomposites are formed on graphene oxide and the antibacterial activity was investigated for possible synergistic effects.
author: Khaleel, Aiman.

title: Synthesis of the Tetrasaccharide GlcNAcLex Fragment of Tumor Associated Carbohydrate Antigen Dimeric Lewis X.
abstract: In living systems, carbohydrates play critical roles in various biological processes, including cell signaling and immunity. It has been observed that tumor cells overexpress abnormal oligosaccharides on their surfaces, known as Tumor-Associated Carbohydrate Antigen (TACAs). The overexpression of these TACAs makes them valuable targets for developing anti-cancer therapeutics. One TACA of interest to our research group is dimLex; a hexasaccharide reported to accumulate in colonic and hepatic carcinomas. Even though dimeric Lex (dimLex ) is tumor specific, it is known that its Lex trisaccharide fragment is displayed at the surface of many healthy cells. Recently, our group discovered that monoclonal antibodies SH2 and 1G5F6 raised against dimLex and polymeric Lex, respectively, retained binding to the dimLex tetrasaccharide fragment GlcNAc- Lex while anti-Lex mAb SH1 did not recognize this tetrasaccharide. Therefore, this tetrasaccharide is particularly interesting in our search for TACA-based cancer immunotherapeutics targeting dimLex. This thesis describes the total synthesis of the novel of GlcNAc-Lex as hexyl and aminohexyl glycosides. The diversity of these two tetrasaccharides will allow conjugation to a carrier protein, analysis by isothermal titration microcalorimetry and nuclear magnetic resonance techniques. The work presented here is a significant contribution to developing potential anti-dimlex therapeutics.
author: Mutoni, Esperance.

title: Synthesizing Fast Weathering Silicate Minerals and Doping with Micronutrients for Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Sequestration through Mineral Carbonation Pathway.
abstract: Mineral carbonation is one of the promising carbon capture, storage and utilization (CCUS) technologies, possessing enormous potential of capturing atmospheric CO2 (a greenhouse gas) and converting it into environmentally stable products. The present research investigates the synthesis process of fast-weathering akermanite (found in steel slags) and calcio-olivine (clinker material in cement industry) using solid-state sintering methodology, dope them with plant nutrients, and investigate the weathering capabilities of these minerals. The term 'fast-weathering' indicates faster leaching of calcium and magnesium cations from the silicate minerals, potentially capturing atmospheric CO2 quickly. Another reference for fast-weathering can be made with the minerals that can capture CO2 at a pace that is in accordance with the 2050 goals of reducing GHG emissions. In addition to the land-based carbon capture solution, the present research investigates the potential production of sodium bicarbonate as an oceanic carbon capture solution. The idea is to synthesize sodium bicarbonate by co-utilization of brine and point-of-source industrial CO2 streams, and by modifying the Solvay process with replacement of ammonia with other buffering additives. X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, BET, and ICP-MS are used to conduct characterization and performance analytics of synthesized minerals. Both akermanite and calcio-olivine were doped with plant nutrients and were synthesized successfully. The weathering experiments indicated the release of calcium and magnesium cations, where fast-weathering silicate minerals showed faster release of alkaline earth metal cations, creating more opportunities for capturing atmospheric CO2. ICP-MS results also indicated the release of potassium cations confirming the nutrients taking part in the weathering process. A machine learning regression model was trained to forecast the weathering rates of minerals based on their composition. It predicted an improved weathering rate for calcio-olivine than akermanite. For oceanic carbon capture solution, the modified Solvay process resulted in the production of sodium bicarbonate with highest quality and quantity of sodium bicarbonate production, while using ammonium bicarbonate as the buffering additive. The results provided research insights and future research directions in the land- and oceanic-carbon capture areas filling potential knowledge gaps for the scientific community.
author: Ali, Asif.

title: Targeted Degradation of Intracellular Proteins using Ubiquitin Variant Induced Proximity.
abstract: Eukaryotic cells rely on proteasomal degradation mechanisms to maintain protein homeostasis, quantity, and quality, essential for regulating various biological processes. A recent trend in drug development involves harnessing the ubiquitin (Ub)-mediated proteolysis pathway for the targeted degradation of disease-causing proteins. One promising approach in this domain is the use of Proteolysis Targeting Chimeras (PROTACs), which are heterobifunctional molecules that contain a module to recruit E3 ligases, a target-binding module, and a linker, thereby directing the target protein to E3 ligases for ubiquitination and subsequent proteasomal degradation. Despite the progress in chemical PROTACs, limited information is available on peptide-based degraders. Previous research in our laboratory has focused on engineered Ub variants (UbVs) as inhibitors and activators of Ub related enzymes, targeting protein-protein interaction surfaces that are often elusive to chemical inhibitors. Building upon this foundation, we hypothesized that UbVs could serve as fusion protein components to facilitate targeted protein degradation-a concept we term UbV induced proximity (UbVIP). The UbVIP approach presents several advantages over small-molecule PROTACS, primarily due to its ability to bind protein-protein interaction sites on target proteins that small molecules cannot access and the utilization of novel E3 ligases. In this PhD thesis, I explore the application of UbVIP technology for intracellular proteins. Chapter 2 of this thesis demonstrates the successful degradation of the target protein, 53BP1, by employing UbVIP to recruit the E3 ligases RFWD3 and NEDD4L. Chapter 3 investigates the effectiveness of recruiting the E2 conjugating enzyme, UBE2B, in degrading target proteins. Collectively, the findings from this research shed light on a peptide-based targeted protein degradation strategy and provide compelling evidence for the exploitation of novel E2 conjugating enzymes and E3 ligases in the ubiquitination and degradation of intracellular proteins. The UbVIP approach holds great promise as a valuable tool in drug development and therapeutic interventions for a wide range of diseases.
author: Aminu, Bayonle.

title: Targeting Notch Signalling To Improve Osteosarcoma Response To Chemotherapy.
abstract: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary bone cancer in humans with localized disease survival rates of approximately 75%, which drops significantly after recurrence and/or metastasis. Cancer stem cell (CSC) theory posits that CSCs represent a small portion of cancer cells but can regenerate the bulk of the tumour, possess chemo-resistance mechanisms and have enhanced migration/metastatic capabilities. One potential target which has been shown to help maintain CSCs is the Notch signalling pathway. MK-0752 is a small molecule gamma secretase inhibitor that blocks the Notch signalling pathway. To investigate whether inhibition of Notch signaling can improve chemotherapy response, commercially available cell lines were grown in conventional monolayer and as spheroids, which enrich for CSCs. Gene expression confirmed the spheroids were a viable model for CSCs. MK-0752 and doxorubicin effect on cell viability and gene expression changes was evaluated. The role of spheroid media in enriching for CSC phenotype was also examined.
author: Russell, Aidan.

title: Taxonomic resolution and the implementation of molecular methods for benthic invertebrate identifications in bioassessment.
abstract: Benthic invertebrates are integral to healthy freshwater ecosystems and are widely used as bioindicators in water quality assessments. This thesis examines two components of Environmental Effects Monitoring assessments of these communities: whether trait responses to effluent are phylogenetically conserved and whether molecular methods can achieve the same or more sensitive results as morphological methods. In the phylogenies of benthic invertebrates, most taxa demonstrated moderate but significant conservatism of tolerances. However, this trait was not conserved at the family level but at lower taxonomic levels such as genus or species. Molecular methods performed similarly to morphological methods at the same taxonomic level in assessing differences in invertebrate communities at reference areas and areas receiving effluent. A greater variation in results was found when assessments used species proxies. This thesis demonstrates the need for higher taxonomic resolution when identifying benthic invertebrates, which can be achieved by implementing molecular methods.
author: Dutheil, Laura.

title: Teichoic acid-associated surface charge impacts Staphylococcus aureus host adhesion.
abstract: Staphylococcus aureus is a highly adaptive pathobiont capable of colonizing and infecting many sites within the human body, resulting in different host clinical outcomes. An early and important step in bacterial colonization and infection is adhesion to the host, which is essential for S. aureus pathogenesis. The research in this thesis explores the role of FmtA in S. aureus host adhesion. The teichoic acid (TA) D-alanine (D-Ala) esterase, FmtA, was previously found to have an uncharacterized role in S. aureus adhesion by the Cox Lab. TAs are cell wall polysaccharides decorated with D-Ala moieties that alter the overall and intrinsic negative charge associated with the TA backbone. FmtA hydrolyses the ester bond between positively charged D-Ala residues and the TA backbone. Modification of TA charge impacts overall cell surface charge, influencing many cellular processes. Using cell surface charge and adhesion assays, the importance of surface charge in S. aureus host adhesion was uncovered. Further investigation revealed that TA charge also plays a role in the spatial regulation of the cell wall-anchored (CWA) Staphylococcal protein A (SpA) adhesin. Overall, I propose that S. aureus can dynamically regulate cell surface charge in response to different environments to enable optimal adhesion, promoting infection and colonization. The FmtA-associated adhesion phenotype is likely due to two factors: a more anionic cell surface charge and reduced surface display of CWA adhesins.
author: Verhoef, Mackenzie.

title: Temperature Influences Phenotypic and Biofilm Changes in Mono- and Co-cultures of Streptococcus suis and Glaesserella parasuis.
abstract: Streptococcus suis and Glaesserella parasuis are common commensals of the tonsils in healthy swine but can also be responsible for outbreaks of severe disease. The purpose of this study was to characterize phenotypic changes in S. suis and G. parasuis in mono- and co-culture in vitro. Planktonic and biofilm co-culture experiments indicated that the clinical S. suis serovar 9 had a fitness advantage over G. parasuis. It was also found that increased temperature could induce drastic changes in colony morphology and significant increases in biofilm biomass of all S. suis strains tested. It was also shown that extracellular matrix components were variable between S. suis strains. Our results suggest that S. suis may have morphological changes at increased temperatures that may promote polymicrobial interactions. Furthermore, clinical S. suis serovar 9 strains have unique biofilm characteristics that may be important for virulence potential that is not seen in serovar 2.
author: Spoja, Brandon.

title: Temperature-Phased Biological Hydrolysis for Anaerobic Digestion of Sewage Sludge: Performance and Microbiology.
abstract: Anaerobic digestion (AD) has been widely used to stabilize wastewater sludge and recently, reinvigorated due to its capacity to recover energy from wastewater sludge. To enhance energy recovery, biological hydrolysis (BH) is used as a pre-treatment method before the AD of wastewater sludge to degrade cellular materials into soluble organics for methane production. The BH temperature and retention time are the two pivotal operating parameters in the processes of BH and BH-AD. Numerous studies have explored the impact of these two parameters on sludge solubilization and methane yield. However, the effect of temperature and retention time of BH stage and AD stage on microbial communities, the characterizations of digestate water, and the linkage among the BH-AD conditions, performance, and dominant microorganisms are still not fully understood. The lack of such knowledge has limited our capacity to understand process mechanisms, identify key process indicators, and develop methods for further energy recovery and performance enhancement. The main objectives of this Ph.D. research were to explore the optimal BH and AD temperature and retention time, develop an extensive understanding of the effect of combined BH temperature and retention time on the microbial consortium of BH and AD stages, and connect the microbial community to the operational conditions, methane, and digestate water quality. The main contributions of this thesis include: (1) determining the optimal temperature and retention time of BH and AD stages for wastewater sludge digestion, (2) revealing the deterministic role of microbial interaction at combined BH temperature and retention time conditions, (3) characterized the soluble microbial products and methane production at different BH and AD temperature conditions, and (4) analyzed the correlations among the BH and AD temperature condition, microbial community, and digestate water quality using principal component and canonical correlation analyses. Therefore, this Ph.D. thesis developed an improved understanding of the effect mechanisms of the BH temperatures and the BH retention times on the BH-AD performance and digestate quality.
author: Wang, Meiying.

title: Temporal Dynamics of Freshwater Mussel Larvae.
abstract: Unionids, which have parasitic glochidia larvae, likely compete for host fishes in multispecies mussel beds (> 20 species). The timing of glochidia abundance and the potential for partitioning of their 'host-fish' environment were examined. Glochidia were continuously sampled at 2-h intervals over ten 24-h days using a rosette sampler in the Sydenham River, Ontario from late August to September 2020. A total of 6104 glochidia from 17 unionid species were analyzed. A generalized linear mixed model indicated that the highest abundance of glochidia was at night, peaking at 20:00 (± 1 h) LST. Chi-square analysis revealed that many species were not uniformly distributed throughout the day, rather, their abundance aligned with reported diel activity patterns of their hosts. Glochidia abundance peaked at different times in unionids that shared hosts, suggesting host partitioning. Results provide insight into the concept of host partitioning to reduce competition, which will aid in unionid conservation.
author: Smodis, Stephanie.

title: Temporal Fusion Transformers: A Novel Approach to Streamflow Prediction.
abstract: Streamflow prediction has traditionally depended on physical-based models. However, recent developments in data-driven machine learning models present promising alternatives for hydrology. This thesis explores these approaches and introduces the Temporal Fusion Transformer model for streamflow prediction, which requires minimal input data and can adapt to various prediction scenarios.To train this model, hydrological data from thousands of North American locations over several decades were combined with climate and land cover data. The model was incrementally developed to demonstrate the strengths of various data sources, hydrological modeling, and additional model enhancements. Initial results reveal the model's exceptional performance in hindcasting across diverse climates, as well as short-term forecasting capabilities when tested in Ontario.However, challenges remain in forecasting ungauged locations and predicting streamflows beyond the short term due to uncertainties in weather forecasting data. Despite these limitations, this research highlights the potential of transformer models in hydrological applications and paves the way for further investigations into improving their predictive capabilities.
author: French, Steven.

title: Temporal Regulation and Subcellular Localization of Alkaline/Neutral Invertase of Developing Zea mays Endosperm: Insights into Sugar Signaling and Carbon Allocation in Cereal Grains.
abstract: Sucrose is the main form of transportable carbohydrate in plants. Sucrose production, transport and breakdown is central to plant growth and development and carbohydrate storage. Sucrose hydrolysis occurs via two enzymatic pathways, sucrose synthase and invertase (INV) and is essential for directing carbon into various metabolic pathways and for sugar signaling. Two different families of INV exist based on their pH optima and subcellular localization, acid INV and alkaline-neutral INV (A/N-INV). A/N-INV have been a lot less studied in comparison to acid INV. In this study, gene expression was used to identify temporal regulation of 10 maize A/N-INV isoforms in developing maize seeds. Localization of INVAN8, a plastidial form of A/N-INV, using isolated plastids and characterization of a recombinant INVAN8 protein were performed to unravel a new function of A/N-INV in carbon metabolism of non-photosynthetic plastids (amyloplast) of developing maize endosperm.
author: Belanger, Maude.

title: Testing Pathogenicity of Neopestalotiopsis spp. in Strawberries F1 Hybrids of Ontario.
abstract: Strawberries are a popular fruit grown commercially in Ontario, with an annual farm gate value of $34 million. Neopestalotiopsis sp. is a new and emerging pathogen that causes a devastating disease in strawberries. Chemical control methods and resistant germplasm are currently unavailable for this emerging pathogen. Commercial fields located in Ontario were monitored for disease incidence, and three distinct species within the genus were found, including Neopestalotiopsis sp., Neopestalotiopsis rosae, and Neopestalotiopsis clavispora. Detached leaf pathogenicity testing demonstrated that N. sp. was the most aggressive, with an average lesion diameter 33% larger than the other species. The popular Ontario cultivar day-neutral 'Albion' was more susceptible to N. sp. than June-bearing 'Jewel.' Screening day neutral F1 hybrids from the University of Guelph strawberry breeding program using detached leaf assays found line 20-047 resistant to all three species of Neopestalotiopsis. Resistance testing using whole plant challenge assays supported the finding that line 20-047 was resistant to N. sp. with an average lesion diameter significantly smaller than 'Albion' (p<0.05, N=30). Further exploration of broad-spectrum resistance in line 20-047 and the development of a diagnostic to differentiate between aggressive N. sp. and less aggressive N. rosae and N. clavispora will benefit the strawberry industry.
author: McNally, Justin.

title: Testing for Structural Change in Higher Moments/Tail Probabilities: Application to Crop Insurance, Climate Change and Production Loss.
abstract: Insurance is primarily interested in lower tail probabilities and as such the detection of structural change in tail probabilities or higher moments is of great concern for the pricing and efficacy of crop insurance programs. I propose a test for structural change with an unknown break(s) which has power against structural change in any moment and can be tailored to a specific range of the underlying distribution if desired. Simulations demonstrate that the proposed test has better finite sample performance to existing methods and performs reasonably well at identifying the break. The asymptotic distribution is shown to be the same form as a Kolmogorov distribution. My proposed test finds structural changes in most major U.S. field crop yields leading to significant premium rate differences. Results of an out-of-sample premium rating game indicate that incorporating structural change in crop yields leads to more accurate premium rates.Climate change is one of the most significant and pressing challenges affecting various aspects of our lives and has a profound impact on the agricultural sector. Changes in tail probabilities capture more complex changes in the distribution of climate variables. Using the proposed test, I examine the presence of structural change in tail probabilities of temperature-based measures and precipitation data across the United States. My findings reveal compelling evidence of significant tail structural change in both temperature and precipitation data and most notably, I find greater evidence of structural change in the tails than I do if I consider the entire distribution.This study further investigates the extent to which climate change contributes to changes in the distribution of crop yields beyond the mean. I assess the presence of structural changes beyond the mean at the county level and target specifically the lower tails of yield distribution. Subsequently, I analyze the resulting p-values and decisions to reject the null hypothesis in relation to changes in key climate variables. The findings reveal that climate change is associated with structural change in the higher moments of the crop yields under examination, even when specifically focusing on the lower tail of the distributions.
author: Lu, Hanjun.

title: Text to Image Synthesis from Scene Descriptions with a Focus on Relative Positioning.
abstract: Text-to-Image Synthesis (also known as text to image generation) refers to the process of generating images given some input description or caption. While traditional scene generation tools used databases of objects and programmed rules for placement, modern deep-learning systems can infer details about an image from images and their captions only. Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) deep learning architectures have demonstrated the ability to generate scenes in the form of two-dimensional bitmap images after learning from a set of similar scenes. Models capable of generating detailed images conditioned on complex scene structure within linguistic descriptions but struggle to internalize inter-object relative position information such as 'to the right and above' and 'close to.' We attempt to demonstrate that a conditional GAN can be trained to generate 2D bitmap representations of simple 2D scenes with a focus on relative positioning relationships matching those in the input linguistic description.
author: Kazmi, Arslan.

title: The 'G' in 'Glamping': Exploring the Key Factors Contributing to Canada and U.S. Glamper's Satisfaction and Glampsite Rebooking Intentions.
abstract: Glamping, a trendy outdoor hospitality product, has received minimal academic attention. The purpose of this thesis is to explore the key predictors of North American glampers' satisfaction and rebooking intention. Meanwhile, the study reviewed and picked five constructs (physical environment, interaction, outcome, access, and enjoyment) and proposed an exploratory conceptual framework. An online panel survey was employed with 423 valid samples collected for a quantitative analysis. Results suggest that both the quality of the physical environment and accessibility predict glampers' satisfaction. Furthermore, accommodation settings could influence the prediction effects of physical environment quality and the interaction quality. The study also conducted a cluster analysis based on two open-ended questions. Concept maps generated by Leximancer demonstrated a comprehensive picture of more contributors to glamping satisfaction and rebooking intention. Findings of the study provide theoretical implications for future glamping studies as well as managerial inspirations for glampsite owners.
author: Ma, Wenxiao.

title: The Affordances of Digital Agricultural Technologies.
abstract: Digital agriculture - a suite of technologies that aim to collect, collate, and analyze large amounts of data - holds the potentially to substantially impact the way food is produced. While digital agriculture technologies have the purported potential for increasing food production while minimizing impacts on the environment, there is a need to explore their social impacts. Looking beyond the intended uses of digital agricultural technologies, this thesis explores their affordances - how their qualities and properties define potential uses - with a particular focus on practice and power. I seek to understand the ways that these technologies support (and challenge) on-going trends in agriculture including increasing adoption of technology, redefining potential uses of digital tech, increasing financialization, and adaptation to climate change. The mixed methods approach of this thesis evaluates these interrelated themes with both a political economy and science and technology studies lens. I reveal the ways that digital agricultural technologies are used in practice and assert that they are configuring power dynamics in new ways for the food systems, highlighting examples from the Canadian context. Overall, I conclude that the social consequences of the various affordances of digital agricultural technologies hold a series of challenges that need to be addressed through interventions to create a sustainable and equitable food system.
author: Duncan, Emily.

title: The Angle Degeneracy Phenomenon in Deep Neural Networks: Analysis and Relation to Training Dynamics.
abstract: Deep neural networks have proven to be powerful functions with many applications, but the theoretical behaviour of these functions is not fully understood. One such behaviour is the large depth degeneracy phenomenon, where inputs tend to become highly correlated as they travel deeper into a randomly initialized network. This can make the network effectively incapable of distinguishing between inputs, which has negative impacts on training performance. Through combinatorial expansions, we develop precise formulas to predict the expected value and variance of the angle between inputs at any layer of the initialized network. We provide a detailed analysis of how quickly the angle tends toward zero in a finite width setting, which proves to be qualitatively different than studying the problem in the infinite width limit. We validate our theoretical results through comparison to empirical simulations, and run experiments to explore how network degeneracy can impact training dynamics.
author: Jakub, Cameron.

title: The Apparent Total-Tract Nutrient Digestibility, Fecal Microbiome, and Fecal Metabolome in Adult Healthy Dogs Fed an Experimental Plant-Based Diet Compared to a Conventional Animal-Based Diet.
abstract: Entirely plant-based diets for dogs are increasing in popularity. However, research on the impact of these diets on gastrointestinal health is limited. The research presented in this thesis included three studies: a apparent total-tract nutrient digestibility trial, a fecal microbiome trial, and a fecal metabolome trial to compare an experimental plant-based diet to a conventional animal-based diet in 61 client-owned healthy adult dogs.The plant-based diet was found to have comparable crude protein, crude fat, and dry matter ATTD to the dogs fed the animal-based diet. The plant-based diets showed the potential to change the canine fecal microbiota, but these changes were distinct from those reported in human literature. Furthermore, the plant-based diet increased fecal metabolites mainly associated with carbohydrate digestion, this supporting previous research into dogs' adaptation to digest and metabolize carbohydrates. The findings of this research provide the basis of future investigation into the impacts of plant-based diets on canine health.
author: Liversidge, Brooklynn.

title: The Application of Silver Nanoparticles and Bacteriophage to Combat Pseudomonas aeruginosa Biofilms.
abstract: Pseudomonas aeruginosa causes 51,000 infections in hospitalized patients and 2,500 deaths annually in the United States alone. Chronic wound infections typically exist as biofilms. Silver nanoparticles- a typical treatment for chronic wound infections- and bacteriophage- viruses that infect bacteria- are a novel area of research to explore the efficacy of biofilm degradation of P. aeruginosa biofilms. Five prospective phages were isolated from the environment and screened against a panel of P. aeruginosa clinical isolates. Pseudomonas strain PA14 was chosen for downstream characterization experiments which included: efficiency of plating, genome annotation, bacteriophage insensitive mutant frequency, adsorption, and one-step growth curve. The selected phage, Chandler, showed similar efficacy to a cocktail of phages and was chosen for downstream experiments such as the minimum biofilm eradication concentration (MBEC). MBEC and imaging results using scanning electron microscopy demonstrated that sequential and combined application of both treatments displayed high biofilm degradation against PA14 biofilms.
author: Kirst, Sarah.

title: The Biologically Effective Doses of Flumioxazin and Pyroxasulfone and their Interactions for Control of Multiple Herbicide-Resistant Waterhemp (Amaranthus tuberculatus (Moq.) J. D. Sauer) in Soybean (Glycine max (L.) Merr.) in Ontario.
abstract: Three studies consisting of eighteen small-plot field trials were completed in 2016 and 2017 to determine the biologically effective doses of flumioxazin and pyroxasulfone applied preemergence and to classify their interactions when applied as a mixture for the control of multiple herbicide-resistant (MHR) waterhemp in soybean. The predicted doses of flumioxazin to provide 90% MHR waterhemp control were 59, 100, 147, and 151 g ai ha−1 at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks after application (WAA), respectively. The predicted doses of pyroxasulfone to provide 90% MHR waterhemp control were 88, 145, 247, and 274 g ai ha−1 at 2, 4, 8, and 12 WAA, respectively. The co-application of pyroxasulfone and flumioxazin primarily resulted in an additive increase in MHR waterhemp control; however, pyroxasulfone (268 g ai ha-1) plus flumioxazin (211 g ai ha-1) resulted in a synergistic increase in waterhemp control and a synergistic decrease in waterhemp shoot biomass at 4 WAA. The co-application of pyroxasulfone and flumioxazin caused a synergistic increase in soybean injury at 2 weeks after emergence; the injury observed was transient.
author: Ferrier, James.

title: The Biomechanics of Locking Plates used in a Supercutaneous Fashion.
abstract: Fracture fixation has progressed from perfect anatomic reduction to fracture stabilization preserving surrounding soft tissue. External fixators cause minimal soft tissue disruption during placement/removal. Despite these benefits, external fixators are bulky and costly leading to the investigation of alternatives. Locking plates, such as the string of pearls (SOP) and locking compression plate (LCP), gain strength from the screw-plate interface. Although soft tissue disruption is required for placement on the bone, precise contouring is not necessary. The present study compares the LCP and SOP placed 20mm off the bone surface to the external fixator. Constructs were tested in cyclic loading and compression to failure. The external fixator was statistically significantly stiffer (p=0.0050) than the LCP and SOP when placed supercutaneously. Additionally, the SOP was statistically stiffer than the LCP (p=0.0050). Thus, veterinary surgeons must carefully consider the implications of using locking in lieu of an external fixator.
author: Proctor, Ashley.

title: The Blyth Festival Theatre and the Imagined Rural Canada.
abstract: Since 1975, the Blyth Festival Theatre has produced a full summer season of Canadian plays, with an emphasis on new work which 'put[s] all Canadians and certainly rural Canadians centre stage' (Blyth Festival Theatre). In response to the theatre's fiftieth anniversary, this dissertation looks back at the Blyth Festival's performance history to track and examine the theatre's on-going representations of rural Canada on its stages. This study is created to add to the important decolonial work by BIPOC and white settler theatre artists, scholars, and activists such as Jill Carter, Cheryl Foggo, Rinaldo Walcott, Dylan Robinson, Cheryl Thompson and others who are critically engaging with colonial histories and retelling obscured or lost stories from equity deserving communities across Turtle Island. Throughout the Blyth Festival's fifty year history, important productions have challenged past representations of the 'imagined community' of rural Canada (Anderson 8). The dissertation's chapters dig into key moments in Blyth Festival's 'performance of the imagined community' of rural Canada which confront previous depictions and/or erasures of rural settlers, settler women, Black settlers, and Indigenous people on the theatre's stage (Filewod Performing Canada 1).
author: Graham, Taylor.

title: The COVID-19 Pandemic Remodeled as a Syndemic Driven by Health and Social Disparities.
abstract: COVID-19 disproportionately affects racial minority groups and those with non-communicable diseases. Overlap in disease epidemics caused by social inequities sparked conversations about a COVID-19 syndemic. A syndemic occurs when the interaction between biological disease states and social environment causes a synergistic increased burden of negative health outcomes to cluster within a population, place, or time. Cumulative COVID-19 morbidity and mortality for the first three epidemic waves in Ontario were modeled as a syndemic using multilevel models. Interaction terms between proxy measures of racial marginalization and COVID-19 comorbidities were included to model the hypothesized synergistic biosocial interaction that increases the burden of COVID-19. The theorized syndemic effect varied by wave and appeared to be strongest during the second wave. Further, the syndemic effect seems to have had a larger impact on COVID-19 morbidity in comparison to mortality. These findings highlighted the importance of addressing racial marginalization to better Ontario's population health.
author: Jeyabalan, Thiyaana.

title: The Case for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Cases of Infectious Enteric Disease.
abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) presents a significant global health challenge, particularly in the realm of infectious enteric diseases, where its impact and driving factors remain inadequately elucidated. This thesis aimed to investigate antimicrobial prescribing patterns in cases of infectious enteric diseases and assess the influence of AMR on disease severity and the overall burden of illness, focusing on cases of nontyphoidal Salmonella. This thesis employed a diverse array of methodological approaches and data sources. This included conducting a scoping review which revealed that a considerable percentage (median value of 30%, with an interquartile range of 11-53%) of infectious enteric disease cases received antimicrobial prescriptions. This finding was reinforced by an analysis of laboratory-confirmed cases in Canada, where more than half (50.6%) reported antimicrobial prescription. Moreover, subsequent analysis found evidence to suggest there may be a considerable amount of inappropriate antimicrobial use in treating laboratory-confirmed enteric disease cases. Transitioning from exploring the drivers of antimicrobial resistance to assessing its impacts on human health, stochastic modeling estimated that in Canada there were over 7,000 illnesses and 140 hospitalizations attributed to resistant domestically-acquired non-typhoidal Salmonella annually between 2015 and 2019. These resistant infections were associated with an annual direct medical cost of 2.93 million CAD. Lastly, analysis revealed that resistant infections of nontyphoidal Salmonella displayed higher odds of prolonged hospital stays (≥3 days) compared to non-resistant cases, albeit without statistical significance. These findings underscore the necessity for antimicrobial stewardship in the context of infectious enteric diseases, emphasizing the imperative to optimize antimicrobial prescribing practices. Ultimately, this thesis demonstrates the potential for improvement in antimicrobial prescribing practices within the realm of infectious enteric diseases and provides compelling evidence regarding the urgent need for stewardship programs, highlighting the profound impacts of AMR in this domain.
author: Dougherty, Brendan.

title: The Cellular Source of Cardiac-Derived Erythropoietin Expression.
abstract: Emerging evidence indicates that within the cardiovascular system, erythropoietin (Epo), has important non-erythropoietic functions in health and disease states. However, it is unknown if the heart is a producer of Epo, or if these effects occur due to circulating Epo. To study the functions of cardiac-derived Epo, a cardiomyocyte-specific Epo deletion mouse was created using cre/lox technology. Interestingly, this cardiomyocyte-specific deletion leads to an upregulation, not loss, of whole heart Epo expression. Using cellular sorting, we found that the remaining cardiomyocytes (~20% of the cardiomyocyte population where the Epo gene has not been deleted) were responsible for producing the Epo overexpression. Together, this thesis identifies the cardiomyocyte cells as an important source of Epo, and establishes that cardiac Epo expression is regulated in a distinct, tissue-specific manner.
author: Holjak, Emma.

title: The Confirmation and Characterization of Synthetic Auxin Herbicide Resistance in a Population of Amaranthus powellii S. Watson from Ontario, Canada.
abstract: Following the suspected evolution of MCPA resistance in a population of green pigweed (Amaranthus powellii) from Dresden, Ontario, experiments were conducted to confirm resistance to synthetic auxin herbicides (SAHs). Dose response experiments were conducted to determine whether the GR50 (herbicide dose causing a 50% reduction in biomass) values were significantly different between the suspected resistant (seed lot accession AMAPO 501) and a known susceptible green pigweed population (seed lot accession AMAPO 511) following application with 2-methyl-4-chloro-phenoxyacetic acid (MCPA), 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), mecoprop, dichlorprop-p, dicamba, halauxifen-methyl, and aminocyclopyrachlor. Results confirmed resistance to MCPA, mecoprop, dichlorprop-p, and aminocyclopyrachlor with resistance factors of 4.4, 2.4, 2.5, and 3.0, respectively. A field study evaluating control with SAHs confirmed resistance; MCPA controlled this population 30% at 8 weeks after application (WAA). To identify alternative herbicide options, four field studies were conducted to evaluate the control of the resistant green pigweed with preemergence (PRE) and postemergence (POST) herbicides in soybean and corn. In soybean, the most efficacious soil-applied and postemergence herbicides were pyroxasulfone/flumioxazin PRE (95% control) and glyphosate POST (94% control) at 8 WAA. In corn, the most efficacious soil-applied and postemergence herbicides were rimsulfuron + mesotrione PRE (88% control) and atrazine POST (94% control) at 8 WAA. Following confirmation of resistance to SAHs, the molecular mechanism of resistance to MCPA was investigated further. Non-target site resistance mechanisms including absorption, translocation, and metabolism were compared between populations 501 and 511; and the results indicated no significant differences in these mechanisms. A gene expression analysis study was conducted using RNA Sequencing. The major differentially expressed genes were linked to a probable target site modification conferring resistance and included 1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylate synthase and SAUR36. Further investigation determined, a single nucleotide polymorphism causing a leucine to phenylalanine substitution in the PB1 domain of auxin response factor 9 (ARF9). The mutation could affect the interaction between Aux/IAA repressor proteins and ARF9 which maintains the repression of auxin responsive genes. Although the full resistance mechanism remains to be elucidated it can be concluded that the mechanism of resistance may be linked to a target site modification.
author: Aicklen, Isabelle.

title: The Cultural Evolution of Fake News: How Exaggeration, Reputation and Cognitive Reflection Impact the Transmission of Information.
abstract: Misinformation is a significant challenge in contemporary life. It can undermine public understanding, health decisions, and trust in institutions while contributing to polarization. In this doctoral dissertation, I used three factors to understand the transmission of fake and true news: cultural evolution, reputation and cognitive reflection. Specifically, cultural evolution, along with broader evolutionary perspectives, informs the entire dissertation and offers a comprehensive examination of information-sharing behaviour. Certain features of a message increase its chance of transmission. Manuscript 1 assessed whether the degree of exaggeration affected the chances of headlines being transmitted using a serial transmission protocol. Exaggerated information was more likely to be transmitted than non-exaggerated information. Manuscript 2 aimed to reduce the sharing of misinformation by using warning messages to prompt cognitive reflection and reputational concerns. I incorporated reputational concerns, as misinformation can harm a person's reputation, which can affect their social status and ability to associate with others. Overall, warning messages can reduce the spread of fake and true news, but their effectiveness may vary depending on the type of news. More specifically, warning messages reduced the sharing of fake but not true political news. However, they decreased the sharing of both true and fake non-political news. Manuscript 3 assessed the impact of shared content on social media on people's reputations and others' willingness to associate with them. Participants rated accounts with shared news as having more negative traits, fewer positive traits, less autonomous thinking, and fewer social interactions than accounts that did not share news. These ratings were more pronounced for an account that shared fake news. Additionally, participants showed less willingness to associate with accounts that shared fake news. In sum, exaggerated information spreads more than non-exaggerated content. Warning messages can trigger reputational concerns, which leads to reduced fake news sharing to some extent, especially for political news. Finally, people perceive those sharing fake news more negatively compared to those sharing other content. If we understand which factors impact the transmission of information, we can better inform the literature and design future interventions to create a more reliable social media environment.
author: Nascimento de Oliveira, Angélica.

title: The Development of Potent and Specific Inhibitors for Smurf1 and Smurf2.
abstract: E3 ubiquitin ligases catalyze protein ubiquitination by facilitating the transfer and subsequent covalent attachment of ubiquitin to a variety of target substrates. This process is essential for normal cellular functions, making E3s attractive therapeutic targets. The HECT subfamily of E3 ubiquitin ligases are involved in many important biological processes such as cancer development, immune function, hypertension among others. Two Smurf (Smad ubiquitin regulator factor) E3 ligases (Smurf1 and Smurf2), are heavily involved in crucial cellular processes such as DNA damage response, gene expression, chromatin organization and cell migration/invasion. Developing synthetic modulators of Smurf proteins is of critical importance to better understand their roles in human health and disease.Recently, a combinatorial structure-based protein engineering strategy has been used to produce ubiquitin variants (UbVs) to manipulate enzymes in the ubiquitination pathway. The goal of this work aimed to identify potent and specific UbVs to inhibit Smurf proteins. This research will lead to the better understanding of Smurf proteins and potentially lead to the development of small-molecule inhibitors and therapeutics targeting Smurf proteins.
author: Stekel, Zane.

title: The Diffusion of Innovation on Subscription-Based Services: The Role of Institutional Works on The Diffusion of Wireless Telephony.
abstract: This research explores the evolution and diffusion of subscription-based wireless telephony services among major Canadian providers, focusing on how Bell, Rogers, and Telus adapted to economic, technological, and competitive changes over 36 years (1984-2020). By applying the Generalized Bass Model and integrating Diffusion of Innovation & Institutional Theory, this study analyzes the forces driving transformation in the telecommunications industry. Comparative analysis of diffusion parameters reveals the absence of institutional isomorphism in a competitive environment, supported by a review of 60 annual financial reports and an examination of managerial strategies and industry trends from 2000 to 2020. The findings indicate that the firms exhibited differentiated diffusion patterns, driven by strategic interventions at critical periods.
author: Bhela, Puneet.

title: The Digitalization of Agriculture and the (Un)Changing Dynamics of Rural Smallholder Farming Systems in Ghana, Sub-Sahara Africa.
abstract: This dissertation explores the dynamics of agricultural digitalization in rural smallholder systems in Northern Ghana, Sub-Sahara Africa, with a focus on the 1) anticipated impacts, 2) nature of farmers' engagement, 3) factors that affect participation, 4) resulting changes to practices, and 5) the elements for successful digital futures. Given the novelties of digitalization, my multidisciplinary scholarly interests and exploratory intentions, I applied political-economy and social practices theoretical and analytical lens, with a mixed-method approach, combining document review, interviews, focus group discussions, observation, and surveys to address the objectives. I found that the digitalization of smallholders is being promoted as transformative by development actors (with potential neoliberal goals) without recourse to the embedded political-economic consequences. Yet, empirical results from Northern Ghana revealed superficial engagement by farmers, as participation and activeness in digital services are structurally hampered by low (digital) literacies and limited access to (digital) resources. Meanwhile, gender, phone ownership, ability to place phone calls, association with farm groups, and access to extension services influence the likelihood of participating and benefiting from digitalization services; however, critical inequities exist across these factors. Nonetheless, digital services may change livelihood practices (everyday routines and space-time rhythms) of farmers as new patterns of actions in season planning, how and when farmers plant, undertake husbandry activities, harvest, market and sell outputs emerge. Hence, I conclude that smallholder digitalization is uncritical detached from farmers' lived realities. While prospects fodigital innovations to transform livelihoods may exist, the basic building blocks are so lacking that this potential is unlikely to be realized in the near term. Meanwhile, uncritical implementation of these tools is only likely to entrench existing inequities and create newer unfair power distributions. Thus, it is essential to move beyond the holistic propagation of digital innovations and the uncritical claims of transformativeness. Instead, we need a context-based 'digitalization for smallholders' that (re)focus the expectations towards incremental change in everyday social practices embedded in (African) farmers' socio-political-economic realities. Thus, stakeholders must work towards inclusive digital access in smallholder systems, partly through establishing and integrating required materials, competencies, and meanings that bring digitalization to life across scales.
author: Abdulai, Abdul-Rahim.

title: The Early Settlement Experiences of Syrian Families in Southwestern Ontario.
abstract: After the Syrian conflict, thousands of refugees fled to neighbouring countries, while fewer travelled to different regions worldwide. Within Canada, 54,560 Syrian refugees were resettled by 2019 (Bose, 2020, P. 14), with Southwestern Ontario welcoming many of these refugees. Various researchers have highlighted different aspects of the experiences of Syrian refugee families in the Canadian context. However, my research explores the experiences of Syrian refugee families during early settlement: employment, education, housing, building relationships, and health. I also looked at household and social reproduction, including the factors that influence social reproductive activities within Syrian households in different-sized communities and via different sponsorship types. Using Nvivo analysis of 38 interviews on early settlement experiences in twelve Southwestern different-sized communities provided insight into the lived experience of these families in general and Syrian women in particular. This research highlights the marginalized voices regarding settlement, helping us to understand the Syrian refugee families' early experiences while helping to identify policy options that would help alleviate challenges and hasten their full integration into their respective communities. بعد الصراع السوري، فر آلاف اللاجئين إلى البلدان المجاورة، في حين هاجرعدد أقل إلى مناطق مختلفة في جميع أنحاء العالم. من ضمنها كندا، بحلول عام 2019 تم استقبال وتوطين 54،560 لاجئًا سوريًا (Bose, 2020,p.14)، وجنوب غرب أونتاريواستقبلت العديد من هؤلاء اللاجئين. سلط باحثون الضوء على جوانب مختلفة من تجارب عائلات اللاجئين السوريين في السياق الكندي. لكن البحث الحالي يستكشف تجارب أسر اللاجئين السوريين خلال اقامتهم المبكره: التوظيف والتعليم والإسكان وبناء العلاقات والصحة. ايضا تم التركيز على الاسره - بما في ذلك العوامل التي تؤثر على الأنشطة الاجتماعية داخل الأسر السورية في المجتمعات المختلفة الحجم وعبرمختلف أنواع الكفالات. في هذا البحث تم تحليل 38 مقابلة مع العائلات السورية وخاصة مع النساء حول تجارب اقامتهم المبكرة في اثنتي عشر مجتمع مختلفة الحجم بحيث قدمت نظرة ثاقبة على التجربة المعيشية لهذه الأسر بشكل عام والنساء السوريات بشكل خاص. يسلط هذا البحث الضوء على الأصوات المهمشة فيما يتعلق باللجؤ الي جنوب غرب اونتاريو، مما يساعدنا على فهم التجارب المبكرة للاجئين السوريين مع المساعدة في تحديد خيارات السياسة التي من شأنها أن تساعد في تخفيف التحديات وتسريع اندماجهم الكامل في مجتمعاتهم المحلي.
author: Telfah, Rana.

title: The Ecology of Place-Based Impact Investing: Examining Place-Based Impact Investing Ecosystems and Enabling Environments in Non-Metro Canada.
abstract: Non-metro Canada is under tremendous pressure, navigating a global pandemic, rising cost of living, inflation, a collapsing healthcare system, skilled labour and housing shortages, supply chain issues, and the impacts of climate change. The current economic, business, and finance systems are built on capital accumulation. These systems perpetuate exploitative and extractive markets that deepen inequity, systemic racism, environmental degradation, social and economic injustice. Place-based approaches provide context for complex and interconnecting issues at a local level; by providing a place-based lens, interventions can be targeted, leveraging investment capital to create impact outcomes for the communities they serve. Place-based approaches also leverage community assets, connecting networks, skills, knowledge, and opportunities for co-creation and collaboration. However, critical to the viability, depth of impact, and sustainability of place-based investment are supportive ecosystems. Place-based impact investing ecosystems are complex, living systems that can be responsive to and influence the environments in which they exist. Since the 1980s, neoliberal economic restructuring, devolution of programs and services, and precarious funding and financing has eroded non-metro resilience. This has directly impacted the ability for non-metro communities to have robust and healthy enabling environments for place-based impact investing ecosystems. This research uses a grounded constructivist methodology to examine place-based impact investing ecosystems and enabling environments in non-metro Canada. The contributions of this work address gaps in literature on the understanding of place-based impact investing ecosystems, the composition, and the enabling forces in non-metro Canada. This research also contributes to understanding the opportunities and limitations of current policy, and the role that coherent policy has in developing enabling environments. Place-based impact investing ecosystems can be described as a complex living system. A model examining the ecology of place-based impact investing ecosystems emerged from this research, and contributes to understanding multi-level macro, meso, and micro influencing factors of place-based impact investing ecosystems and enabling environment development. The place-based impact investing ecosystem and enabling environment (PIIE) model contributes to the field by situating a macro-level understanding of the influence of dominant political ideologies on policy development, global markets on investment, the influences of globalization on (national, provincial, and municipal) economies, and the role that sustainable development has in fostering the broader development of impact investing through social innovation. The meso-level provides an opportunity to analyze pressures created by political ideologies on policy development, including influence on the development of enabling environments, the economies where place-based impact investing and ecosystem development exist, and the social innovation that cultivates development of tools used to address complex challenges. The micro-level situates the three main components essential for place-based impact investing ecosystem development. Place is at the centre of PIIE, illustrating the most essential component-the intent to create impact in the communities they serve.
author: Allen, Katie.

title: The Effect of Blood Flow Restriction Training on Rock Climbing Performance, Forearm Blood Oxygenation and Cardiovascular Responses.
abstract: Blood flow restriction (BFR) training has been shown to affect local and central cardiovascular adaptations to exercise, but there has been limited investigation of sport-specific performance applications. To investigate select peripheral muscle and vascular physiological responses to BFR training, a group of rock climbers were selected to compare the effect of post-climbing BFR hangboard training during a 6-week intervention study, to climbing alone. Climbing performance and a handgrip test evaluating hemodynamics, and the exercise pressor response (EPR) to contractions and post-exercise circulatory occlusion (PECO) were investigated. Following training, time to task failure on the climbing test (using the Kilter Board) in the BFR and NBFR group increased significantly by 34.1% (P = 0.0003) and 27.6% (P=0.004). Both groups showed improvements in forearm hemodynamics which could relate to the improvements in climbing performance, however, changes in the EPR were inconsistent. The addition of BFR training does not further enhance performance.
author: Mckellar, Ben.

title: The Effect of Dietary Lysine Supply on Mammary Gland Development During Late Gestation in Gilts and Multiparous Sows.
abstract: Insufficient milk production for large litters can be combatted by increasing mammary gland development, and the amount of milk available per piglet. The objective of this thesis was to investigate the effects of dietary lysine quantity and source for improvement of mammary gland development in late gestating gilts and multiparous sows. When late gestating multiparous sows were provided dietary lysine 40 % above current NRC (2012) recommendations via soybean meal, there were no improvements in mammary parenchymal mass or composition. Thus, multiparous sows do not require additional lysine for mammary gland development, unlike what was reported previously for late-pregnant gilts. Gilts fed dietary lysine up to 30 % below NRC (2012) recommendations via soybean meal, casein, or crystalline lysine did not experience detrimental effects to mammary development, regardless of lysine source. Therefore, parity and goals of the herd should be considered when assessing the lysine quantity in gestating sow diets.
author: Gillies, Caroline.

title: The Effect of Environmental and Animal Characteristics on Cattle Behaviour at Rest-Stops During Long-Distance Transportation.
abstract: This thesis studied the effects of long-distance transport on the lying, eating, and drinking behaviour of cattle unloaded at two commercial rest stations. First, we characterized loads of cattle (n=89) stopping at rest stations, documenting load, trip, rest station, and animal handling characteristics. Loads with feeder (47.2%), weaned (27.0%), market weight (11.2%), culled beef cows (11.2%), and breeding (3.4%) cattle were examined. We observed market weight cattle travelling west, which had not been documented previously. The mean transport time (26 h) and mean duration at the rest station (13 h) showed compliance above and beyond the federal humane transport regulations. We observed differences in truck compartment sizes compared to previously published standard values, which can impact space allowance calculations. The effects of providing straw bedding, load, trip, and rest station characteristics on lying, eating, and drinking behaviour were examined at the group level. Cattle prioritized eating in the initial hours after unloading, lying down afterwards suggesting feelings of hunger may predominate over any feelings of fatigue. We observed two significant interactions with bedding: bedding resulted in cattle lying down sooner; and the probability of lying increased with longer truck's time in motion in bedded pens and decreased in non-bedded pens. We examined associations between provision of bedding, load, trip, and rest station characteristics and different characteristics of lying behaviour (i.e., odds of observing lying behaviour, lying posture, latency, duration, number of lying bouts and bout duration) for individual animals. We observed significant interactions between bedding and two independent variables: cattle with bedding laid down sooner, with trips over 15 h in motion; and heavier cattle laid down for less time in bedded pens. Finally, we described and assessed the effects of load, trip, rest station, and animal characteristics on cattle waterer use at one rest station. The waterer was used almost immediately after unloading and its use was observed more often soon after unloading. Drinker use decreased with longer time in motion. Providing bedding changed certain aspects of cattle's behaviour which may be associated with different affective states that could have an effect on their welfare.
author: Olivares Guzman, Paula.

title: The Effect of Gradual Ovarian Failure on Dynamic Muscle Function and the Role of a High Intensity Interval Training Intervention.
abstract: Skeletal muscle contractile function is impaired in menopause and exercise may mitigate this decline. I used the VCD model of menopause to investigate the effects of gradual ovarian failure on skeletal muscle contractile function and whether high intensity interval training (HIIT) can mitigate impairments. Sexually mature female CD-1 mice were assigned to one of three groups: control sedentary (n=5), VCD-sedentary (n=5), or VCD-training (n=5). Following ovarian failure (a 4-month process), the VCD-training group underwent 8 weeks of uphill HIIT. Mice were sacrificed 8 weeks after ovarian failure, representing late menopause. Single fibres from the soleus (SOL) and extensor digitorum longus (EDL) muscles were dissected, chemically permeabilized, and mechanically tested. Single muscle fibres were maximally activated (pCa 4.5), then isotonic load clamps were performed to evaluate force-velocity-power relationships. Absolute force and peak power were 31% & 32% lower in VCD-sedentary fibres compared to control fibres, respectively, in both SOL and EDL muscles. Despite reductions in absolute force and therefore lighter relative loads imposed during the isotonic contractions in VCD-sedentary fibres, there were no concomitant increases in contractile velocity to preserve power production. HIIT attenuated force loss in the VCD-training group such that it was not different from the control group across muscles, and was partially effective at mitigating power loss (22% higher peak power in VCD-training compared to VCD-sedentary), but only in fast-type SOL fibres. These findings indicate that ovarian failure impairs dynamic contractile function - likely through a combination of lower force-generating capacity and slower shortening velocity - and that HIIT may be insufficient to completely counteract the deleterious effects of menopause at the cellular level.
author: Hubbard, Emma.

title: The Effect of Intimacy: Comparing Criminal Justice Outcomes for Child Homicide.
abstract: Globally, and in Canada, child homicides account for a small portion of the total number of homicides; however, few crimes carry the same level of moral outrage due to the victims' vulnerability. Child homicides are most likely to be perpetrated by individuals in a parental role with the victim which is commonly referred to as filicide. But, as children age, they are more likely to be killed by strangers or perpetrators with whom they have a non-familial relationship. While the effect of victim perpetrator relationship on criminal justice outcomes has been explored in the context of homicide, assault, and violent offences, its effect on child homicide cases remains largely unexplored in the Canadian context. To address this gap, this dissertation focused on child homicides that occurred in Ontario between 1985 and 2018 and applied an explanatory sequential mixed-method design to examine the effect of victim perpetrator relationship on criminal justice outcomes across three studies. The first study investigated whether criminal justice responses to child homicide vary by degree of intimacy between victims and perpetrators and found that familial perpetrators were less likely to be convicted of murder. Using a focal concerns lens, it explored how stereotypes about intimacy and violence may impact the criminal justice outcomes perpetrators with a familial relationship with their victim receive. The second study explored whether criminal justice outcomes vary for male and female perpetrators of filicide and found maternal perpetrators received less serious criminal justice outcomes compared to paternal perpetrators. To explore why these differences may occur, the third study conducted a reflexive thematic analysis of sentencing transcripts using an integrated social role and medicalization perspective to compare judicial discussions of maternal and paternal filicide perpetrators. The developed themes demonstrated not only differences between maternal and paternal filicide, but also differences in how judges discussed the filicidal acts of maternal and paternal perpetrators. Overall, these results highlight the need for future researchers, legal actors, and policymakers to develop a more comprehensive understanding of the effect of intimacy, gender and parenthood stereotypes, and non-legal factors on criminal justice decision-making in child homicide cases.
author: Johnson, Anna.

title: The Effect of Low Anatomical Temperatures on Oncolytic Rhabdoviruses.
abstract: Cancers and infectious diseases are two of the most significant health problems globally and new therapeutics and prevention methods are being researched every day. Viral vectors are powerful tools that can be used to treat cancers and in the prevention of infectious diseases, in the form of oncolytic viruses (OVs) and viral vector-based vaccines, respectively. Rhabdoviruses have shown efficacy in in vitro and in pre-clinical studies as viral vectors. However, research investigating how environmental conditions affect their performance when used as therapeutics is limited. Preliminary data from our research group demonstrated that decreased temperatures could negatively affect the oncolytic ability of two rhabdoviruses, vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV) and Maraba virus. This thesis aimed to further investigate the relationship between lower temperatures and viral vector efficacy and how cold adaptation could be used as a mitigation strategy. The interface between virus biology, cell biology, and temperature is also discussed and investigated.
author: Kakish, Julia.

title: The Effect of Surface Complexity and Organic Matter on Crop Yield in Southern Ontario, Canada, with Consideration for Precipitation, Crop Type, and Soil Texture.
abstract: Crop yield varies spatially and temporally due to variability in topography, soil properties, climate, and other factors. This research evaluated the effect of surface complexity and soil organic matter (SOM) on yield, and compared this relationship for different crop types, soil texture, and seasonal precipitation. Surface complexity explained between 0-62% of yield variability. The strongest relationship between surface complexity and yield was found on fields where corn was grown on moderately coarse textured soil, and when precipitation was below average. Similarly, SOM explained between 0-57% of crop yield variability. SOM also had the strongest effect on yield variability when corn was grown in moderately coarse soils, and when precipitation was below average. This suggests that both surface complexity and SOM are important variables in understanding crop variability when water supply is low. This information highlights the importance of SOM and how crops will respond to future changes in climate patterns.
author: Burton, Erin.

title: The Effects of 5α-androstane-3α,17β-diol (3α-diol) Treatment on Neuropathology and Cognitive Dysfunction in the 3xTg Mouse Model of Alzheimer's Disease
abstract: Age-related decreases in gonadal steroid hormone levels have been associated with an increased risk of developing Alzheimer's disease (AD). The incidence of AD is higher in women than men, which may in part reflect the neuroprotective effects of testosterone. Recent in vitro studies have suggested that 5α-reduced neurosteroid metabolites of testosterone, including 5α-androstane-3α,17β-diol (3α-diol), may contribute to these neuroprotective effects and observed sex differences in AD. This thesis investigated whether supplementation with 3α-diol might confer protection against the development of AD in the 3xTg mouse model of AD. 3α-diol treatment improved long-term OR memory in 3xTg-AD males but not females, significantly increased hippocampal dendritic spine density, and differentially altered male and female CA1 dendritic branching and length. The findings presented in this thesis provide novel evidence that 3α-diol treatment exerts sex-specific neuroprotective effects in 3xTg-AD mice.
author: Kuwahara, Nariko.

title: The Effects of Acute Stress and Peripheral Inflammation on the Male Rat Hippocampus.
abstract: Stress and inflammation can interfere with the normal structure and function of neurons within the hippocampus. Physical stress can induce remodelling of hippocampal dendrites associated with impaired hippocampal-dependent memory. Furthermore, the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and the immune system are highly interconnected and work to attenuate the effects of peripheral injury. Stress and glucocorticoids can regulate peripheral and neuroinflammatory responses by exerting immunosuppressive or immune-enhancing effects on immune cells and cytokines in the circulation and hippocampus. While glucocorticoid exposure before the activation of inflammation can prime glia within the hippocampus, acute stress after immune challenge can suppress peripheral and neuroinflammatory responses. However, while much of the current literature focuses on the chronic effects of stress and inflammation, the acute responses within the hippocampus following peripheral injury have received less attention. The studies presented in this thesis explore the rapid effects of stress and inflammation on hippocampal inflammation and structural plasticity using the Complete Freund's Adjuvant (CFA) model of inflammatory injury. Chapter 2 of this thesis demonstrated the early truncation of dendrites on pyramidal neurons within the hippocampus CA3 region following CFA-induced inflammatory injury while circulating glucocorticoids and molecular markers of neuroinflammation remained at control levels. In Chapter 3, the transient response of circulating glucocorticoids and dorsal CA3 dendritic structure was observed in the first hours and days following CFA-induced inflammatory injury. This thesis represents the first characterization of hippocampal dendritic structure following peripheral injury. Collectively, these findings highlight the ability of the hippocampus to recover from stress and inflammation and understanding the acute stress response following peripheral inflammatory injury. Our findings of the transient response of corticosterone and dendritic structure present important implications for understanding of underlying morphological changes in hippocampal neurons during the recovery from inflammatory injury.
author: Isaacs, Lauren.

title: The Effects of Arginine on Gene Expression in Bovine Mammary and Longissimus Dorsi Tissues.
abstract: The objective of this study was to determine effects of Arg supply on the expression of a select set of genes related to AA metabolism, vascular function, cellular regulation of protein synthesis, and cell differentiation and turnover between the mammary and longissimus dorsi tissues. Six rumen-cannulated, lactating Holstein cows were used in a replicated 3 × 3 Latin square design with 14-d periods. Treatments were continuous abomasal infusion that supplied 0 (ARGx0), 49.2 (ARGx1), and 98.4 (ARGx2) g/d Arg in an otherwise complete AA mixture at 1054 g/d. Arg appeared to support milk production and maintain the protein synthesis setpoint in the mammary glands through up-regulation of genes related to NEAA synthesis, AA transport, and protein synthesis regulators, whereas gene expression in muscle was consistent with an optimized response to Arg supply and supported the milk production setpoint by acting as a buffer to supply additional AA to the mammary glands.
author: Fox, Madison K.
